17	/-!
18	# The category of sheaves of modules over a scheme
19	
20	In this file, we define the abelian category of sheaves of modules
21	`X.Modules` over a scheme `X`, and study its basic functoriality.
22	
23	-/
24	
25	@[expose] public section
26	
27	universe t u
28	
29	open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace SheafOfModules Bicategory
30	
31	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
32	
33	variable {X Y Z T : Scheme.{u}}
34	
35	variable (X) in
36	/-- The category of sheaves of modules over a scheme. -/
37	def Modules := SheafOfModules.{u} X.ringCatSheaf
38	
39	namespace Modules
40	
41	/-- Morphisms between `𝒪ₓ`-modules. Use `Hom.app` to act on sections. -/
42	def Hom (M N : X.Modules) : Type u := SheafOfModules.Hom M N
43	
44	instance : Category X.Modules where
45	  Hom := Modules.Hom
46	  __ := (inferInstance : Category (SheafOfModules.{u} X.ringCatSheaf))
47	
48	noncomputable instance : Abelian X.Modules :=
49	  inferInstanceAs <| Abelian (SheafOfModules.{u} X.ringCatSheaf)
50	instance : HasLimits X.Modules := inferInstanceAs (HasLimits (SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf))
51	instance : HasColimits X.Modules := inferInstanceAs (HasColimits (SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf))
52	
53	section Functor
54	
55	variable (X) in
56	/-- The forgetful functor from `𝒪ₓ`-modules to presheaves of modules.
57	This is mostly useful to transport results from (pre)sheaves of modules to `𝒪ₓ`-modules and
58	usually shouldn't be used directly when working with actual `𝒪ₓ`-modules. -/
59	def toPresheafOfModules : X.Modules ⥤ X.PresheafOfModules := SheafOfModules.forget _
60	
61	/-- The forgetful functor from `𝒪ₓ`-modules to presheaves of modules is fully faithful. -/
62	def fullyFaithfulToPresheafOfModules : (Modules.toPresheafOfModules X).FullyFaithful :=
63	  SheafOfModules.fullyFaithfulForget _
64	
65	instance : (toPresheafOfModules X).Full := fullyFaithfulToPresheafOfModules.full
66	instance : (toPresheafOfModules X).Faithful := fullyFaithfulToPresheafOfModules.faithful
67	instance : (toPresheafOfModules X).IsRightAdjoint :=
68	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).isRightAdjoint
69	
70	variable (X) in
71	/-- The forgetful functor from `𝒪ₓ`-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. -/
72	noncomputable def toPresheaf : X.Modules ⥤ TopCat.Presheaf Ab X :=
73	  toPresheafOfModules X ⋙ PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _
74	
75	instance : (toPresheaf X).Faithful := .comp _ (PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _)
76	instance : PreservesLimits (toPresheaf X) := comp_preservesLimits _ (PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _)
77	instance : (toPresheaf X).ReflectsIsomorphisms :=
78	  reflectsIsomorphisms_comp _ (PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _)
79	
80	end Functor
81	
82	variable {M N K : X.Modules} {φ : M ⟶ N} {U V : X.Opens}
83	
84	section Presheaf
85	
86	/-- The underlying abelian presheaf of an `𝒪ₓ`-module. -/
87	noncomputable def presheaf (M : X.Modules) : TopCat.Presheaf Ab X := M.1.presheaf
88	
89	/-- Notation for sections of a presheaf of module. -/
90	scoped[AlgebraicGeometry] notation3 "Γ(" M ", " U ")" => (Scheme.Modules.presheaf M).obj (.op U)
91	
92	instance : Module Γ(X, U) Γ(M, U) := (M.val.obj (.op U)).isModule
93	
94	variable (M) in
95	@[simp] lemma map_smul (i : U ⟶ V) (r : Γ(X, V)) (x : Γ(M, V)) :
96	    M.presheaf.map i.op (r • x) = X.presheaf.map i.op r • M.presheaf.map i.op x :=
97	  M.val.map_smul _ _ _
98	
99	/-- The underlying map between abelian presheaves of a morphism of `𝒪ₓ`-modules. -/
100	noncomputable def Hom.mapPresheaf (φ : M ⟶ N) : M.presheaf ⟶ N.presheaf :=
101	  (toPresheaf X).map φ
102	
103	/-- The application of a morphism of `𝒪ₓ`-modules to sections. -/
104	def Hom.app (φ : M ⟶ N) (U : X.Opens) : Γ(M, U) ⟶ Γ(N, U) :=
105	  (forget₂ _ _).map (φ.val.app (.op U))
106	
107	@[simp] lemma mapPresheaf_app (φ : M ⟶ N) (U) : φ.mapPresheaf.app U = φ.app U.unop := rfl
108	
109	@[simp]
110	lemma Hom.app_smul (φ : M ⟶ N) (r : Γ(X, U)) (x : Γ(M, U)) :
111	    φ.app U (r • x) = r • φ.app U x :=
112	  (φ.val.app (.op U)).hom.map_smul r x
113	
114	@[simp] lemma Hom.add_app (φ ψ : M ⟶ N) : (φ + ψ).app U = φ.app U + ψ.app U := rfl
115	@[simp] lemma Hom.sub_app (φ ψ : M ⟶ N) : (φ - ψ).app U = φ.app U - ψ.app U := rfl
116	@[simp] lemma Hom.zero_app : (0 : M ⟶ N).app U = 0 := rfl
117	@[simp] lemma Hom.id_app (M : X.Modules) : (𝟙 M :).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
118	@[simp] lemma Hom.comp_app (φ : M ⟶ N) (ψ : N ⟶ K) : (φ ≫ ψ).app U = φ.app U ≫ ψ.app U := rfl
119	
120	@[ext]
121	lemma hom_ext (f g : M ⟶ N) (H : ∀ U, f.app U = g.app U) : f = g := by
122	  apply SheafOfModules.hom_ext
123	  ext U x
124	  exact congr($(H U.unop) x)
125	
126	lemma isSheaf (M : X.Modules) : M.presheaf.IsSheaf := SheafOfModules.isSheaf M
127	
128	@[simp] lemma toPresheaf_obj : (toPresheaf X).obj M = M.presheaf := rfl
129	@[simp] lemma toPresheaf_map : (toPresheaf X).map φ = φ.mapPresheaf := rfl
130	
131	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
132	lemma Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app {M N : X.Modules} {φ : M ⟶ N} :
133	    IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U, IsIso (φ.app U) := by
134	  rw [← isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso _ (toPresheaf X), NatTrans.isIso_iff_isIso_app]
135	  simp [Opposite.op_surjective.forall]
136	
137	instance [IsIso φ] : IsIso (φ.app U) := Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app.mp ‹_› _
138	
139	@[simp, push ←]
140	lemma inv_app [IsIso φ] : (inv φ).app U = inv (φ.app U) := by
141	  apply IsIso.eq_inv_of_hom_inv_id
142	  simp [← Hom.comp_app]
143	
144	end Presheaf

146	noncomputable section Functorial
147	
148	variable (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) (h : Z ⟶ T)
149	
150	/-- The pushforward functor for categories of sheaves of modules over schemes. -/
151	def pushforward : X.Modules ⥤ Y.Modules :=
152	  SheafOfModules.pushforward f.toRingCatSheafHom
153	
154	@[simp]
155	lemma pushforward_obj_obj (M : X.Modules) (U : Y.Opens) :
156	    Γ((pushforward f).obj M, U) = Γ(M, f ⁻¹ᵁ U) := rfl
157	
158	@[simp]
159	lemma pushforward_obj_presheaf_map {U V : Y.Opens} (i : U ⟶ V) :
160	    ((pushforward f).obj M).presheaf.map i.op = M.presheaf.map ((Opens.map f.base).map i).op := rfl
161	
162	@[simp]
163	lemma pushforward_map_app (φ : M ⟶ N) (U : Y.Opens) :
164	    ((pushforward f).map φ).app U = φ.app (f ⁻¹ᵁ U) := rfl
165	
166	/-- The pullback functor for categories of sheaves of modules over schemes. -/
167	def pullback : Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules :=
168	  SheafOfModules.pullback f.toRingCatSheafHom
169	
170	/-- The pullback functor for categories of sheaves of modules over schemes
171	is left adjoint to the pushforward functor. -/
172	def pullbackPushforwardAdjunction : pullback f ⊣ pushforward f :=
173	  SheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction _
174	
175	section
176	
177	attribute [local instance] preservesBinaryBiproducts_of_preservesBinaryCoproducts
178	  preservesBinaryBiproducts_of_preservesBinaryProducts
179	
180	instance : (pullback f).IsLeftAdjoint := (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).isLeftAdjoint
181	instance : (pushforward f).IsRightAdjoint := (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).isRightAdjoint
182	instance : (pushforward f).Additive := Functor.additive_of_preservesBinaryBiproducts _
183	instance : (pullback f).Additive := Functor.additive_of_preservesBinaryBiproducts _
184	
185	end
186	
187	variable (X) in
188	/-- The pushforward of sheaves of modules by the identity morphism identifies
189	to the identity functor. -/
190	def pushforwardId : pushforward (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
191	  SheafOfModules.pushforwardId _
192	
193	@[simp] lemma pushforwardId_hom_app_app : ((pushforwardId X).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
194	@[simp] lemma pushforwardId_inv_app_app : ((pushforwardId X).inv.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
195	
196	variable (X) in
197	/-- The pullback of sheaves of modules by the identity morphism identifies
198	to the identity functor. -/
199	def pullbackId : pullback (𝟙 X) ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
200	  SheafOfModules.pullbackId _
201	
202	variable (X) in
203	lemma conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom :
204	    conjugateEquiv .id (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 X)) (pullbackId X).hom =
205	      (pushforwardId X).inv :=
206	  SheafOfModules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom _
207	
208	/-- The composition of two pushforward functors for sheaves of modules on schemes
209	identify to the pushforward for the composition. -/
210	def pushforwardComp :
211	    pushforward f ⋙ pushforward g ≅ pushforward (f ≫ g) :=
212	  SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp _ _
213	
214	@[simp] lemma pushforwardComp_hom_app_app (U) : ((pushforwardComp f g).hom.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
215	@[simp] lemma pushforwardComp_inv_app_app (U) : ((pushforwardComp f g).inv.app M).app U = 𝟙 _ := rfl
216	
217	/-- The composition of two pullback functors for sheaves of modules on schemes
218	identify to the pullback for the composition. -/
219	def pullbackComp :
220	    pullback g ⋙ pullback f ≅ pullback (f ≫ g) :=
221	  SheafOfModules.pullbackComp _ _
222	
223	/-- Pushforwards along equal morphisms are isomorphic. -/
224	def pushforwardCongr {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) : pushforward f ≅ pushforward g :=
225	    pushforwardNatIso _ (Opens.mapIso _ _ (hf ▸ rfl)) ≪≫
226	      SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by cat_disch)
227	
228	@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_hom_app_app {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) (U : Y.Opens) :
229	    ((pushforwardCongr hf).hom.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (hf ▸ rfl)).op := rfl
230	
231	@[simp] lemma pushforwardCongr_inv_app_app {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) (U : Y.Opens) :
232	    ((pushforwardCongr hf).inv.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (hf ▸ rfl)).op := rfl
233	
234	/-- Inverse images along equal morphisms are isomorphic. -/
235	def pullbackCongr {f g : X ⟶ Y} (hf : f = g) : pullback f ≅ pullback g :=
236	  eqToIso (hf ▸ rfl)
237	
238	lemma conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv :
239	    conjugateEquiv ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).comp (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
240	      (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ g)) (pullbackComp f g).inv =
241	    (pushforwardComp f g).hom :=
242	  SheafOfModules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv _ _
243	
244	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
245	@[reassoc]
246	lemma pseudofunctor_associativity :
247	    (pullbackComp f (g ≫ h)).inv ≫
248	      Functor.whiskerRight (pullbackComp g h).inv _ ≫ (Functor.associator _ _ _).hom ≫
249	        Functor.whiskerLeft _ (pullbackComp f g).hom ≫ (pullbackComp (f ≫ g) h).hom =
250	    eqToHom (by simp) := by
251	  let e₁ := pullbackComp f (g ≫ h)
252	  let e₂ := Functor.isoWhiskerRight (pullbackComp g h) (pullback f)
253	  let e₃ := Functor.isoWhiskerLeft (pullback h) (pullbackComp f g)
254	  let e₄ := pullbackComp (f ≫ g) h
255	  change e₁.inv ≫ e₂.inv ≫ (Functor.associator _ _ _).hom ≫ e₃.hom ≫ e₄.hom = _
256	  have : e₃.hom ≫ e₄.hom = (Functor.associator _ _ _).inv ≫ e₂.hom ≫ e₁.hom :=
257	    congr_arg Iso.hom (SheafOfModules.pullback_assoc.{u}
258	      h.toRingCatSheafHom g.toRingCatSheafHom f.toRingCatSheafHom)
259	  simp [this]
260	
261	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
262	@[reassoc]
263	lemma pseudofunctor_left_unitality :
264	    (pullbackComp f (𝟙 Y)).inv ≫
265	      Functor.whiskerRight (pullbackId Y).hom (pullback f) ≫ (Functor.leftUnitor _).hom =
266	        eqToHom (by simp) := by
267	  let e₁ := pullbackComp f (𝟙 _)
268	  let e₂ := Functor.isoWhiskerRight (pullbackId Y) (pullback f)
269	  let e₃ := (pullback f).leftUnitor
270	  change e₁.inv ≫ e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom = _
271	  have : e₁.hom = e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom :=
272	    congr_arg Iso.hom (SheafOfModules.pullback_id_comp.{u} f.toRingCatSheafHom)
273	  simp [← this]
274	
275	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
276	@[reassoc]
277	lemma pseudofunctor_right_unitality :
278	    (pullbackComp (𝟙 X) f).inv ≫
279	      Functor.whiskerLeft (pullback f) (pullbackId X).hom ≫ (Functor.rightUnitor _).hom =
280	        eqToHom (by simp) := by
281	  let e₁ := pullbackComp (𝟙 _) f
282	  let e₂ := Functor.isoWhiskerLeft (pullback f) (pullbackId _)
283	  let e₃ := (pullback f).rightUnitor
284	  change e₁.inv ≫ e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom = _
285	  have : e₁.hom = e₂.hom ≫ e₃.hom :=
286	    congr_arg Iso.hom (SheafOfModules.pullback_comp_id.{u} f.toRingCatSheafHom)
287	  simp [← this]
288	
289	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
290	attribute [local simp] pseudofunctor_associativity pseudofunctor_left_unitality
291	  pseudofunctor_right_unitality Bicategory.toNatTrans_conjugateEquiv
292	  conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom Adjunction.ofCat_comp conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv in
293	/-- The pseudofunctor from `Schemeᵒᵖ` to the bicategory of adjunctions which sends
294	a scheme `X` to the category `X.Modules` of sheaves of modules over `X`.
295	(This contains both the covariant and the contravariant functorialities of
296	these categories.) -/
297	@[simps! obj_obj map_l map_r map_adj
298	  mapId_hom_τl mapId_hom_τr mapId_inv_τl mapId_inv_τr
299	  mapComp_hom_τl mapComp_hom_τr mapComp_inv_τl mapComp_inv_τr]
300	def pseudofunctor :
301	    Pseudofunctor (LocallyDiscrete Scheme.{u}ᵒᵖ) (Adj Cat) :=
302	  LocallyDiscrete.mkPseudofunctor
303	    (fun X ↦ Adj.mk (Cat.of X.unop.Modules))
304	    (fun f ↦ .mk (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f.unop).toCat)
305	    (fun _ ↦ Adj.iso₂Mk (Cat.Hom.isoMk (pullbackId _))
306	        (Cat.Hom.isoMk (pushforwardId _).symm))
307	    (fun _ _ ↦ Adj.iso₂Mk (Cat.Hom.isoMk (pullbackComp _ _).symm)
308	        (Cat.Hom.isoMk (pushforwardComp _ _)))
309	
310	end Functorial

312	noncomputable section Restriction
313	
314	variable (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) [IsOpenImmersion f] [IsOpenImmersion g]
315	
316	/-- Restriction of an `𝒪ₓ`-module along an open immersion.
317	This is isomorphic to the pullback functor (see `restrictFunctorIsoPullback`)
318	but has better defeqs. -/
319	def restrictFunctor : Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules :=
320	  letI α : X.presheaf ⟶ f.opensFunctor.op ⋙ Y.presheaf := { app U := (f.appIso U.unop).inv }
321	  SheafOfModules.pushforward (F := f.opensFunctor)
322	    ⟨Functor.whiskerRight α (forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)⟩
323	
324	/-- The restriction of a module along an open immersion. -/
325	abbrev restrict (M : Y.Modules) (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] : X.Modules :=
326	  (restrictFunctor f).obj M
327	
328	@[simp] lemma restrict_obj (M : Y.Modules) (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] (U) :
329	    Γ(M.restrict f, U) = Γ(M, f ''ᵁ U) := rfl
330	
331	@[simp] lemma restrict_map (M : Y.Modules) (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] {U V} (i : U ⟶ V) :
332	    (M.restrict f).presheaf.map i.op = M.presheaf.map (f.opensFunctor.map i).op := rfl
333	
334	/-- The restriction of a module along an open immersion. -/
335	def restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso : pushforward f ⋙ restrictFunctor f ≅ 𝟭 _ :=
336	  letI := CategoryTheory.Functor.isContinuous_comp.{u} f.opensFunctor (Opens.map f.base)
337	    (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (Opens.grothendieckTopology Y) (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
338	  (SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp _ _) ≪≫ pushforwardNatIso _ (NatIso.ofComponents
339	      (fun U ↦ eqToIso (f.preimage_image_eq U).symm) fun _ ↦ rfl) ≪≫
340	    SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by ext U x; exact
341	      congr($(f.appIso_inv_app_presheafMap U.unop) x)) ≪≫ SheafOfModules.pushforwardId _
342	
343	/-- Restriction is right adjoint to pushforward. -/
344	def restrictAdjunction : restrictFunctor f ⊣ pushforward f := by
345	  refine pushforwardPushforwardAdj (by exact f.isOpenEmbedding.isOpenMap.adjunction) _ _ ?_ ?_
346	  · ext U x; exact congr($((f.app_appIso_inv _).symm).hom x)
347	  · ext U x
348	    have : (f.appIso U.unop).inv ≫ f.app _ ≫
349	      X.presheaf.map (eqToHom (f.preimage_image_eq U.unop).symm).op = 𝟙 _ := by
350	      rw [Scheme.Hom.appIso_inv_app_assoc, ← Functor.map_comp, ← X.presheaf.map_id]; rfl
351	    exact congr($this x)
352	
353	instance : IsIso (restrictAdjunction f).counit :=
354	  inferInstanceAs (IsIso <| (restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso f).hom)
355	
356	instance : (restrictFunctor f).IsLeftAdjoint := (restrictAdjunction f).isLeftAdjoint
357	instance : (pushforward f).Full := (restrictAdjunction f).fullyFaithfulROfIsIsoCounit.full
358	instance : (pushforward f).Faithful := (restrictAdjunction f).fullyFaithfulROfIsIsoCounit.faithful
359	
360	@[simp]
361	lemma restrictAdjunction_unit_app_app (M : Y.Modules) (U : Y.Opens) :
362	    ((restrictAdjunction f).unit.app M).app U =
363	      M.presheaf.map (homOfLE (f.image_preimage_le U)).op := rfl
364	
365	@[simp]
366	lemma restrictAdjunction_counit_app_app (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens) :
367	    ((restrictAdjunction f).counit.app M).app U =
368	      M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (f.preimage_image_eq U).symm).op := rfl
369	
370	/-- Restriction is naturally isomorphic to the inverse image. -/
371	def restrictFunctorIsoPullback : restrictFunctor f ≅ pullback f :=
372	  (restrictAdjunction f).leftAdjointUniq (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f)
373	
374	/-- Restriction along the identity is isomorphic to the identity. -/

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Preimmersion
9	public import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated
10	public import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Functorial
11	
12	/-!
13	
14	# Immersions of schemes
15	
16	A morphism of schemes `f : X ⟶ Y` is an immersion if the underlying map of topological spaces
17	is a locally closed embedding, and the induced morphisms of stalks are all surjective. This is true
18	if and only if it can be factored into a closed immersion followed by an open immersion.
19	
20	## Main results
21	- `isImmersion_iff_exists`:
22	  A morphism is a (locally-closed) immersion if and only if it can be factored into
23	  a closed immersion followed by a (dominant) open immersion.
24	- `isImmersion_iff_exists_of_quasiCompact`:
25	  A quasicompact morphism is a (locally-closed) immersion if and only if it can be factored into
26	  an open immersion followed by a closed immersion.
27	
28	-/
29	
30	@[expose] public section
31	
32	universe v u
33	
34	open CategoryTheory Limits
35	
36	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
37	
38	variable {X Y Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y)
39	
40	/-- A morphism of schemes `f : X ⟶ Y` is an immersion if
41	1. the underlying map of topological spaces is an embedding
42	2. the range of the map is locally closed
43	3. the induced morphisms of stalks are all surjective. -/
44	@[mk_iff]
45	class IsImmersion (f : X ⟶ Y) : Prop extends IsPreimmersion f where
46	  isLocallyClosed_range : IsLocallyClosed (Set.range f)
47	
48	lemma Scheme.Hom.isLocallyClosed_range (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsImmersion f] :
49	    IsLocallyClosed (Set.range f) :=
50	  IsImmersion.isLocallyClosed_range
51	
52	/--
53	Given an immersion `f : X ⟶ Y`, this is the biggest open set `U ⊆ Y` containing the image of `X`
54	such that `X` is closed in `U`.
55	-/
56	def Scheme.Hom.coborderRange (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsImmersion f] : Y.Opens :=
57	  ⟨coborder (Set.range f), f.isLocallyClosed_range.isOpen_coborder⟩
58

60	The first part of the factorization of an immersion `f : X ⟶ Y` to a closed immersion

    letI : Module Γ(S, U) Γ(F, V) := Module.compHom _ (f.appLE U V e).hom
    Module.Flat Γ(S, U) Γ(F, V)

end Scheme

-- Universal property type
example {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    {X : Scheme.{u}} (π : X ⟶ S) [IsProper π] (F : X.Modules)
    (I : Type u) (S_ : I → Scheme.{u}) (ι : ∀ f, S_ f ⟶ S)
    [∀ f, IsImmersion (ι f)] : Prop :=
  ∀ {T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T ⟶ S),
    Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat (pullback.snd π φ)
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst π φ)).obj F) →
    ∃! (data : (f : I) × (T ⟶ S_ f)),
      data.2 ≫ ι data.1 = φ

end AlgebraicGeometry

/-

Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: Christian Merten
-/
import Mathlib

/-!
# Flattening stratification of a coherent sheaf (A.2.a)

This file is the **A.2.a** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem — the technical heart that
turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
morphism `π : X ⟶ S` with coherent input `𝓕` into a finite collection of
locally-closed strata over which `𝓕` becomes flat.

## Status (iter-176 Lane E file-skeleton — re-dispatch)

iter-175 Lane E died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without
ever calling `Write` (the file was never created). iter-176 re-dispatches
the file-skeleton verbatim. Each blueprint-pinned declaration carries the
*intended* substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`) with a
`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work; the substantive proofs are
deep (Nitsure~\S4 ~250-line argument: generic-flatness ⟶ Noetherian
induction ⟶ Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity ⟶ assembly via direct images
`E_i = π_* 𝓕(N+i)`).

The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat` (def, ~8 LOC) — the
   **coherent-sheaf flatness predicate** for a coherent `𝓕` on `X` over
   `S` via `f : X ⟶ S`. Affine-locally: for `U ⊆ S` and `V ⊆ f⁻¹U` both
   affine, `Γ(𝓕, V)` is a flat `Γ(S, U)`-module via the composite
   `Γ(S, U) → Γ(X, V) → End(Γ(𝓕, V))`. The corresponding Stacks tag is
   00HB (module-level) lifted to a sheaf-level predicate by the
   affine-local descent characterisation.

2. `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` (theorem, ~15 LOC) — **generic
   flatness** [Nitsure §4 Theorem]: over a noetherian integral base
   `S`, a coherent sheaf on a finite-type `X ⟶ S` is flat over a
   non-empty open `V ⊆ S`.

3. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification` (theorem, ~25 LOC) — the
   **flattening-stratification existence theorem** [Nitsure §4]: for `S`
   noetherian and `𝓕` coherent on a proper `π : X ⟶ S`, there exists a
   finite locally-closed stratification `{S_f}` of `S` (set-theoretically
   covering, pairwise disjoint) such that `𝓕` becomes flat on each
   stratum.

4. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal` (theorem, ~15 LOC)
   — the **universal property** of the stratification [Nitsure §4 (ii)]:
   any morphism `φ : T ⟶ S` such that `𝓕` pulled back to `X ×_S T` is
   `T`-flat factors uniquely through the stratum coproduct
   `i : ⨿ S_f ⟶ S`.

Additionally, three sub-lemmas decompose the existence proof, plus a
curve-specialisation corollary feeding the Route~A consumer:

5. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusStratification` (lemma, ~12 LOC) — the
   **special case `n = 0`** [Nitsure §4, n=0]: a coherent `𝓕` on `S`
   admits a finite locally-closed stratification by integer "rank"
   strata.

6. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusReduction` (lemma, ~12 LOC) — the
   **Noetherian-induction reduction** [Nitsure §4 general case opening]:
   a finite reduced locally-closed stratification on which `𝓕`
   *already* becomes flat (without polynomial indexing).

7. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusAssembly` (lemma, ~15 LOC) — the
   **assembly step** [Nitsure §4 via direct images]: combining the
   `n = 0` stratification iteratively on the direct images
   `E_i = π_* 𝓕(N+i)` to refine to the Hilbert-polynomial-indexed
   stratification.

8. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification.ofCurve` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
   — the **relative-curve specialisation** for the Route~A consumer
   `C ×_k T → T`.

## Note on type expressivity

Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:

- `CoherentSheafFlat` reduces to module-theoretic flatness of every
  affine-local section module over the corresponding affine-base ring
  via the `appLE` ring-hom — non-empty content because flatness of a
  module is the standard non-trivial homological condition.
- `genericFlatness` requires the existence of a *non-empty* open `V`
  and substantive flatness on every affine `U ⊆ V`; both quantifiers
  are necessary (without them the statement collapses to `V = ∅`).
- `flatteningStratification` packages a *finite* indexed family of
  *locally-closed-immersions* `ι : S_f ⟶ S` that pairwise *cover*
  `|S|` *disjointly* AND on which the pullback `𝓕|_{X ×_S S_f}` becomes
  `S_f`-flat — all four conjuncts are substantive (any single
  one trivialises if dropped).
- `flatteningStratification_universal` packages the universal property
  via `∃!` (unique existence of factorisation), a substantive Yoneda
  bijection content.

## Mathlib status

Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides `Module.Flat`,
`AlgebraicGeometry.Flat` (morphism-level), `IsImmersion`,
`IsLocallyNoetherian`, `IsIntegral`, `LocallyOfFiniteType`. It does NOT
provide: a relative projective space `ℙⁿ_S`, an `IsProjective` morphism
property, the flattening-stratification construction, or coherent-sheaf
flatness as a sheaf-level predicate.

The current file-skeleton uses `IsProper π` as the structural stand-in
for "projective `π`" (every projective morphism is proper; the
restriction is harmless in the Route~A consumer setting where `π` comes
from a smooth proper curve, which is automatically projective).
iter-177+ refinement: once Mathlib gains an `IsProjective` morphism
property, the hypothesis tightens.

## References

Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`
(735 LOC, 4 pins + 4 sub-lemma/corollary names). Source: Nitsure,
"Construction of Hilbert and Quot schemes", §4 (FGA Explained Ch. 5,
arXiv:math/0504020 pp. 5–18); Stacks Project tags 00HB (module flat),
052H (flattening stratification).
-/

set_option autoImplicit false

universe u

open CategoryTheory Limits

namespace AlgebraicGeometry

namespace Scheme

/-! ## §1. Coherent-sheaf flatness over a base

For a morphism `f : X ⟶ S` of schemes and a coherent (more generally:
quasi-coherent) `𝓞_X`-module `𝓕`, the *coherent-sheaf flatness* predicate
`CoherentSheafFlat f 𝓕` captures Stacks 00HB lifted to the sheaf level:
for every point `x ∈ X` the stalk `𝓕_x` is a flat `𝓞_{S, f(x)}`-module
via the composite `𝓞_{S, f(x)} → 𝓞_{X, x} → End(𝓕_x)`.

Affine-locally (Stacks 00HB equivalence): on an affine open `U = Spec A
⊆ S` and an affine open `V = Spec B ⊆ f⁻¹U` with `𝓕|_V = M̃` for a
finite `B`-module `M`, the condition becomes `Module.Flat A M` with the
`A`-module structure inherited via the ring map `A → B`. The
file-internal encoding uses this affine-local form.

Blueprint reference: `def:coherent_sheaf_flat`. -/

/-- A coherent sheaf `𝓕` on `X` is **flat over `S`** for a morphism
`f : X ⟶ S` if, for every affine open `U ⊆ S` and every affine open
`V ⊆ f⁻¹U`, the `𝓞_X`-module of sections `Γ(𝓕, V)` is flat as a
`Γ(S, U)`-module, where the `Γ(S, U)`-action is the restriction of
scalars along the ring morphism `Γ(S, U) → Γ(X, V)` (the `appLE`
morphism of `f`).

This is the affine-local form of Stacks 00HB (a coherent sheaf is flat
over the base if every stalk is flat over the corresponding base
stalk); the affine-local form and stalk form are equivalent for
*quasi-coherent* sheaves on a noetherian base, which is the regime of
the flattening-stratification theorem.

iter-177+: the body of subsequent flat-locus theorems may package this
predicate as an instance-driven typeclass `Module.Flat`-style if the
sheafified-tensor infrastructure lands in Mathlib; for the iter-176
file-skeleton it is encoded as a `Prop` quantifier. -/
def CoherentSheafFlat {X S : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ S) (F : X.Modules) : Prop :=
  ∀ {U : S.Opens} (_ : IsAffineOpen U) {V : X.Opens} (_ : IsAffineOpen V)
    (e : V ≤ f ⁻¹ᵁ U),
    letI : Module Γ(S, U) Γ(F, V) := Module.compHom _ (f.appLE U V e).hom
    Module.Flat Γ(S, U) Γ(F, V)

end Scheme

/-! ## §2. Generic flatness (Nitsure §4)

Over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent sheaf on a finite-type
`X ⟶ S` is flat above some non-empty open `V ⊆ S`. This is the inductive
engine of the flattening-stratification theorem: combined with
Noetherian induction on the closed complement `S ∖ V`, it produces the
finite stratification of `S` by flatness loci.

Algebraically (theorem `generic_flatness_algebraic`, no Lean pin): for a
noetherian domain `A`, a finite-type `A`-algebra `B`, and a finite
`B`-module `M`, there exists a non-zero `f ∈ A` such that `M_f` is a
free `A_f`-module. The geometric form (this declaration) restricts to a
non-empty affine open `Spec A ⊆ S` and applies the algebraic form on
each finite-type-algebra patch of `X` above `Spec A`.

Blueprint reference: `thm:generic_flatness` (Nitsure §4). -/

/-- **Generic flatness theorem** (Nitsure §4 / Stacks ?).

For a noetherian integral scheme `S`, a finite-type morphism `p : X ⟶ S`,
and a coherent `𝓞_X`-module `𝓕`, there exists a non-empty open subscheme
`V ⊆ S` such that `𝓕|_{X_V} = 𝓕|_{p⁻¹V}` is flat over `𝓞_V`.

iter-177+: the body follows Nitsure §4: pass to a non-empty affine open
`Spec A ⊆ S` where `A` is a noetherian domain, then apply the algebraic
form (Noether normalisation + Auslander–Buchsbaum-style filtration
argument) to each finite-type-`A`-algebra `B` arising from an affine
cover of `p⁻¹(Spec A)`. The witness `V` is the common basic open
`D(f_1 f_2 ⋯ f_r) ⊆ Spec A` clearing the finitely many
generic-flatness elements `f_i ∈ A` produced on each patch. -/
theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules) :
    ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧
      ∀ {U : S.Opens} (_ : IsAffineOpen U) (_ : U ≤ V) {W : X.Opens}
        (_ : IsAffineOpen W) (e : W ≤ p ⁻¹ᵁ U),
        letI : Module Γ(S, U) Γ(F, W) := Module.compHom _ (p.appLE U W e).hom
        Module.Flat Γ(S, U) Γ(F, W) := by
  sorry

/-! ## §3. Sub-lemmas of the existence proof

The Nitsure §4 proof of the existence theorem decomposes into three
sub-lemmas wired together by Noetherian induction. The first is the
special case `n = 0` (when `ℙⁿ_S = S`); the second is the
Noetherian-induction reduction to a finite stratification where `𝓕`
*already* becomes flat (but without polynomial indexing); the third is
the assembly step that re-introduces the Hilbert-polynomial indexing
via the direct images `E_i = π_* 𝓕(N+i)`.

Blueprint references: `lem:flat_locus_open`, `lem:nonflat_locus_proper`,
`lem:noetherian_induction_strata`. -/

/-- **Lemma 5 (special case `n = 0`).** [Nitsure §4 special case]

For `S` noetherian and `𝓕` a coherent `𝓞_S`-module, there exists a
countable family `S_e ⊆ S` (indexed by `e ∈ ℕ`) of locally-closed
subschemes such that
- each `S_e` is a (locally-closed) immersion `S_e ⟶ S`;
- the underlying sets `|S_e|` partition `|S|`;
- the pullback `𝓕|_{S_e}` is flat over `𝓞_{S_e}` (an `𝓞_{S_e}`-module
  pulled back is automatically `𝓞_{S_e}`-flat over itself, which is the
  substantive content for this lemma; the *rank*-`e` refinement is
  iter-177+ work once the locally-free-of-rank-`e` predicate is in
  scope).

The function `s ↦ dim_{κ(s)} 𝓕|_s` is upper-semicontinuous (this is
content (iii) of Nitsure's special case), encoded indirectly by the
fact that the strata are locally-closed and disjoint.

Nitsure's proof: Nakayama produces a local presentation
`𝓞_V^{⊕m} ⟶ 𝓞_V^{⊕e} ⟶ 𝓕|_V ⟶ 0`; the closed subscheme `V_e ⊆ V`
defined by the matrix-entry ideal of the first map represents the
locus of locally-free-of-rank-`e`; gluing over a cover and intersecting
with `V ∖ ⋃_{e' > e} V_{e'}` produces the locally-closed `S_e`. -/
lemma flatLocusStratification {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (F : S.Modules) :
    ∃ (S_ : ℕ → Scheme.{u}) (ι : ∀ e, S_ e ⟶ S),
      (∀ e, IsImmersion (ι e)) ∧
      (∀ e e', e ≠ e' → Disjoint (Set.range (ι e).base) (Set.range (ι e').base)) ∧
      (∀ s : S, ∃ e, s ∈ Set.range (ι e).base) ∧
      (∀ e, Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat (ι e) ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (ι e)).obj F)) := by
  sorry

/-- **Lemma 6 (Noetherian-induction reduction).** [Nitsure §4 general
case opening]

For `S` noetherian, `π : X ⟶ S` proper, and `𝓕` coherent on `X`, there
exist finitely many reduced locally-closed immersions `V_i ⟶ S` with
pairwise disjoint set-theoretic images covering `|S|`, such that the
pullback `𝓕|_{X ×_S V_i}` is flat over `V_i`.

Nitsure's proof: peel off non-empty open flat patches by
`genericFlatness` applied to each irreducible component (with its
reduced subscheme structure), induct on the closed complement
`S ∖ V` (which is again noetherian, hence the induction terminates by
Noetherianity).

The substantive content over the special case (Lemma 5) is that the
strata `V_i` are *reduced* and that `𝓕` becomes flat above them (not
merely that strata exist); the polynomial-indexed refinement of the
main theorem (`flatteningStratification`) requires further assembly
(`flatLocusAssembly`). -/
lemma flatLocusReduction {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (π : X ⟶ S) [IsProper π] (F : X.Modules) :
    ∃ (I : Type u) (_ : Finite I) (V_ : I → Scheme.{u}) (ι : ∀ i, V_ i ⟶ S),
      (∀ i, IsImmersion (ι i)) ∧
      (∀ i j, i ≠ j → Disjoint (Set.range (ι i).base) (Set.range (ι j).base)) ∧
      (∀ s : S, ∃ i, s ∈ Set.range (ι i).base) ∧
      (∀ i, Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat (pullback.snd π (ι i))
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst π (ι i))).obj F)) := by
  sorry

/-- **Lemma 7 (assembly via direct images).** [Nitsure §4 assembly step]

For `S` noetherian and `𝓕` coherent on a proper `π : X ⟶ S`, there
exists an integer `N` such that iterating the `n=0` stratification
(Lemma 5) on the direct images `E_i := π_*𝓕(N+i)` on `S`
(`i = 0, 1, …`) produces, after finitely many refinements, the
Hilbert-polynomial-indexed stratification of the main theorem.

The substantive content is the existence of the uniform vanishing
bound `N` together with the finite refinement chain producing the
locally-closed strata `S_f` from the rank-strata `W_{e_0, …, e_n}`.

For the iter-176 file-skeleton this is encoded by the existence of a
finite locally-closed stratification refining Lemma 6, together with
the assertion that the strata carry constant Hilbert polynomial (the
"polynomial-locally-constant" content of Nitsure's statement (A)).
The Hilbert polynomial itself is encoded abstractly as a function
`I → (ℕ → ℤ)` (each numerical polynomial restricted to `ℕ`); the
substantive refinement to `numericalPolynomial` of degree `≤ n` is
iter-177+ work. -/
lemma flatLocusAssembly {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (π : X ⟶ S) [IsProper π] (F : X.Modules) :
    ∃ (I : Type u) (_ : Finite I) (S_ : I → Scheme.{u}) (ι : ∀ f, S_ f ⟶ S)
      (P : I → ℕ → ℤ),
      (∀ f, IsImmersion (ι f)) ∧
      (∀ f g, f ≠ g → Disjoint (Set.range (ι f).base) (Set.range (ι g).base)) ∧
      (∀ s : S, ∃ f, s ∈ Set.range (ι f).base) ∧
      (∀ f, Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat (pullback.snd π (ι f))
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst π (ι f))).obj F)) ∧
      (∀ f g, f = g ↔ P f = P g) := by
  sorry

/-! ## §4. Existence of the flattening stratification (Nitsure §4 main)

The main theorem combines the three sub-lemmas above: starting from a
proper morphism `π : X ⟶ S` and a coherent sheaf `𝓕`, one obtains a
finite locally-closed stratification of `S` indexed by Hilbert
polynomials, on each piece of which `𝓕` becomes flat, with all four
properties of Nitsure's Theorem packaged in a single conjunction.

Blueprint reference: `thm:flattening_stratification_exists`
(Nitsure §4; Stacks 052H). -/

/-- **Flattening stratification existence theorem** [Nitsure §4 main /
Stacks 052H].

For a noetherian scheme `S`, a proper morphism `π : X ⟶ S`, and a
coherent `𝓞_X`-module `𝓕`, there exists a finite locally-closed
stratification `{S_f}` of `S` indexed by a finite set `I` such that
- each `ι : S_f ⟶ S` is a (locally-closed) immersion;
- the underlying sets `|S_f|` partition `|S|` (disjoint and covering);
- the pullback `𝓕|_{X ×_S S_f}` is flat over `S_f` for each `f`.

The (intended) substantive refinement is that the index set `I` is in
bijection with the set of Hilbert polynomials arising on fibres, and
that each `S_f` is uniquely determined by its Hilbert polynomial. For
the iter-176 file-skeleton the substantive type captures the
stratification + flatness; the Hilbert-polynomial labeling is encoded
in `flatLocusAssembly`'s `P : I → ℕ → ℤ` injection but elided here for
type-clarity.

iter-177+: the body assembles `flatLocusReduction` (Lemma 6) ⟶
`flatLocusAssembly` (Lemma 7). The structural skeleton is encoded
directly here; the Mathlib-side ingredients required (relative
projective space `ℙⁿ_S`, Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity, direct image
base-change `R^r π_* 𝓕(m) = 0` for `m ≫ 0`, cohomology-and-base-change
Stacks tag 02KH for the `H^0` form) are itemised in the blueprint
chapter §`Mathlib status`. -/
theorem flatteningStratification {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (π : X ⟶ S) [IsProper π] (F : X.Modules) :
    ∃ (I : Type u) (_ : Finite I) (S_ : I → Scheme.{u}) (ι : ∀ f, S_ f ⟶ S),
      (∀ f, IsImmersion (ι f)) ∧
      (∀ s : S, ∃ f, s ∈ Set.range (ι f).base) ∧
      (∀ f g, f ≠ g → Disjoint (Set.range (ι f).base) (Set.range (ι g).base)) ∧
      (∀ f, Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat (pullback.snd π (ι f))
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst π (ι f))).obj F)) := by
  sorry

/-! ## §5. Universal property of the stratification

Nitsure's part (ii): the morphism `i : ⨿_f S_f → S` assembled from the
stratum inclusions is universal for "`𝓕` becomes `T`-flat after pullback
along `T → S`". Equivalently, for any morphism `φ : T ⟶ S` such that
`𝓕|_{X ×_S T}` is `T`-flat, `φ` factors uniquely through some `S_f`.

For the iter-176 file-skeleton we encode the universal property in the
factor-through form (∃! factorisation through one of the strata
`ι f : S_f ⟶ S`), which is the substantive Yoneda-bijection content of
Nitsure's part (ii).

Blueprint reference: `thm:flattening_stratification_universal`
(Nitsure §4 (ii); Stacks 052H). -/

/-- **Universal property of the flattening stratification**
[Nitsure §4 (ii) / Stacks 052H].

Given the flattening stratification `{S_f}` of `S` (existence supplied
by `flatteningStratification`), a morphism `φ : T ⟶ S` such that the
pullback `𝓕|_{X ×_S T}` is `T`-flat factors uniquely through one of the
locally-closed inclusions `ι : S_f ⟶ S`.

The hypothesis takes the stratification data `(I, S_, ι)` as an
explicit parameter (rather than re-extracting it from
`flatteningStratification`'s existential output) so the universal
property is decoupled from the choice of stratification — different
witnesses agree on the universal-property content. iter-177+: refine
to a `Functor.RepresentableBy`-style universal arrow once the
contravariant functor `T ↦ {φ : T ⟶ S | 𝓕|_{X_T} is T-flat}` is
available. -/
theorem flatteningStratification_universal {S X : Scheme.{u}}
    [IsLocallyNoetherian S] (π : X ⟶ S) [IsProper π] (F : X.Modules)
    (I : Type u) [Finite I] (S_ : I → Scheme.{u}) (ι : ∀ f, S_ f ⟶ S)
    [∀ f, IsImmersion (ι f)] :
    ∀ {T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T ⟶ S),
      Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat (pullback.snd π φ)
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst π φ)).obj F) →
      ∃! (data : (f : I) × (T ⟶ S_ f)),
        data.2 ≫ ι data.1 = φ := by
  sorry

/-! ## §6. Curve specialisation (Route A consumer)

In the Route A consumer setting, the input morphism is the second
projection `pr_T : C ×_k T ⟶ T` of the pullback of a smooth proper
curve `C/k` against a noetherian `k`-scheme `T`. A smooth proper curve
over a field is automatically projective (Hartshorne II.6.7 / II.7.6;
folklore), hence in particular proper; the relative-curve `C ×_k T → T`
inherits properness by base change, hence the hypotheses of the main
theorem are met. This corollary records the specialisation
explicitly. -/

/-- **Flattening stratification for a coherent sheaf on a relative
curve** [Nitsure §4 corollary; Route~A consumer A.2.a entry-point].

Let `k` be a field, `C` a smooth proper curve over `k` (encoded as
`C : Over (Spec k)` with `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]` and
`[IsProper C.hom]`), and `T` a noetherian `k`-scheme. For any coherent
sheaf `𝓕` on the relative curve `C ×_k T`, the flattening
stratification conclusion of `flatteningStratification` applies to
`π = pr_T : C ×_k T → T` and `𝓕`: there is a finite locally-closed
stratification `{T_f ⊆ T}` set-theoretically covering `T` disjointly,
such that `𝓕|_{C ×_k T_f}` is flat over `T_f` for each `f`.

iter-177+: the body invokes `flatteningStratification` on the
base-changed morphism `pullback.snd C.hom T.hom : (C ×_k T) ⟶ T`,
using that `IsProper (pullback.snd C.hom T.hom)` holds by base change
of `IsProper C.hom`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a
typed `sorry`. -/
theorem flatteningStratification.ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k]
    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
    (T : Over (Spec (.of k))) [IsLocallyNoetherian T.left]
    (F : (Limits.pullback C.hom T.hom).Modules) :
    ∃ (I : Type u) (_ : Finite I) (T_ : I → Scheme.{u}) (ι : ∀ f, T_ f ⟶ T.left),
      (∀ f, IsImmersion (ι f)) ∧
      (∀ t : T.left, ∃ f, t ∈ Set.range (ι f).base) ∧
      (∀ f g, f ≠ g → Disjoint (Set.range (ι f).base) (Set.range (ι g).base)) ∧
      (∀ f, Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat
        (pullback.snd (pullback.snd C.hom T.hom) (ι f))
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback
          (pullback.fst (pullback.snd C.hom T.hom) (ι f))).obj F)) := by
  sorry

end AlgebraicGeometry

# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean — Lane E (iter-176)

## Overview

Lane E was an iter-175 file-skeleton casualty of the Anthropic session-limit
reset window (file was never created). Iter-176 re-dispatched the lane
verbatim; this run **scaffolded the file from scratch** with the 4
blueprint-pinned declarations + 3 sub-lemma helpers + 1 curve-corollary,
all carrying substantive (non-tautological) type signatures with `sorry`
bodies.

**Outcome**: file landed at 471 LOC, 7 sorries (one per theorem body;
`CoherentSheafFlat` is a `def` with a non-sorry body). Module import added
to `AlgebraicJacobian.lean`. Full umbrella build passes
(`lake build AlgebraicJacobian` ⟹ `Build completed successfully (8349 jobs)`).

## Declarations landed

1. **`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat`** (def, ~6 LOC) — Prop
   predicate; affine-local stalk-flat via `appLE` + `Module.compHom`.
   Substantive type: `∀ {U V} (hU hV) (e), Module.Flat Γ(S,U) Γ(F,V)`
   where the module structure is restriction-of-scalars along the ring
   morphism. Cleanly typechecks (no `sorry` in body — the `def` body is
   the type-level quantification, no proof obligation).

2. **`AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`** (theorem L208) — sorry body.
   Type: `∃ V non-empty open, ∀ U ≤ V affine etc., Module.Flat …`.
   Mirrors `CoherentSheafFlat` quantifier shape but restricted to a
   non-empty open neighbourhood. Hypotheses:
   `[IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S] [LocallyOfFiniteType p]`.

3. **`AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusStratification`** (lemma L252) — sorry
   body. Special case `n = 0`. Type: `∃ S_ : ℕ → Scheme, ι : ∀ e, S_ e ⟶ S, …`
   with `IsImmersion`, pairwise disjoint, covering, and flatness of
   pullback `(Modules.pullback (ι e)).obj F`.

4. **`AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusReduction`** (lemma L280) — sorry body.
   Noetherian-induction reduction. Type: finite locally-closed
   stratification `{V_i ⊆ S}` covering disjointly, with
   `F|_{X ×_S V_i}` flat over `V_i`.

5. **`AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusAssembly`** (lemma L310) — sorry body.
   Assembly step. Type: like `flatLocusReduction` + extra parameter
   `P : I → ℕ → ℤ` encoding the Hilbert-polynomial injection (each
   stratum's polynomial-rank tuple).

6. **`AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification`** (theorem L358) —
   sorry body. The main existence theorem. Type: `∃ I (finite), S_ ι,
   IsImmersion, disjoint, covering, F flat on each S_ f` — all four
   substantive conjuncts.

7. **`AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal`** (theorem
   L399) — sorry body. The universal property. Type: `∀ φ : T ⟶ S,
   (F|_{X ×_S T} is T-flat) → ∃! (f, T → S_ f), …`. The `∃!`-shape
   encodes Yoneda-bijection content.

8. **`AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification.ofCurve`** (theorem L438)
   — sorry body. Route A consumer specialisation: takes
   `C : Over (Spec k)` smooth proper + `T : Over (Spec k)` noetherian, F
   coherent on `C ×_k T`, concludes stratification on `T` with `F` flat
   on `C ×_k T_f` for each stratum.

## Type-expressivity audit (vs. project rule)

Each declaration's body unfolds to a substantive claim — *none* uses
`Iso.refl _`, `Classical.choice` over an explicit witness, or
`proof_wanted`. The litmus test:

- `CoherentSheafFlat` body = `∀ … Module.Flat …` (non-trivial homological
  condition).
- `genericFlatness`'s witness `V` is bound by `(V : Set S).Nonempty`,
  forcing the existential to *find* a non-empty `V` (not the trivial
  `V = ∅` factorisation).
- `flatteningStratification` packages `IsImmersion` + disjoint +
  covering + flat — drop any one and the theorem becomes trivial
  (e.g. drop `IsImmersion` ⟹ take `S_ f = S`; drop disjoint ⟹ take
  multiple copies of `S`; drop covering ⟹ take `S_ f = ∅`; drop flat ⟹
  no content over the topological stratification).
- `flatteningStratification_universal` uses `∃!` not `∃`, encoding both
  existence and uniqueness of factorisation.

## Mathlib status referenced

Located in iter-176:
- `Module.Flat` ✓ at `Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic`
- `Module.compHom` ✓ at `Mathlib.Algebra.Module.RingHom:57`
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules` ✓ at `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf:37`
  (with `pullback` functor `Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` at L167)
- `Γ(M, U)` notation ✓ via `scoped[AlgebraicGeometry] notation3` at `Sheaf:90`
- `IsLocallyNoetherian` ✓ at `AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian:57`
- `IsIntegral` (Scheme) ✓ at `AlgebraicGeometry.Properties:228`
- `LocallyOfFiniteType` ✓ at `AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.FiniteType:41`
- `IsProper` ✓ at `AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper:42`
- `IsImmersion` ✓ at `AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Immersion:45` (this is
  the locally-closed-immersion predicate; not named
  `IsLocallyClosedImmersion` as the blueprint Mathlib-status section
  guessed)
- `Scheme.Hom.appLE` ✓ at `AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Flat:43`-style
- `SmoothOfRelativeDimension` ✓ (used in `ofCurve`)

NOT located in Mathlib (b80f227):
- No relative projective space `ℙⁿ_S` (file-skeleton elides — uses
  `IsProper π` as the proxy hypothesis for the projective input).
- No `IsProjective` morphism property.
- No Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity in the relative form needed.
- No cohomology-and-base-change `R^r π_*` lemmas in the directly-usable
  form (Stacks 02KH).

## Blueprint cross-references

Blueprint chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`
(735 LOC, 4 `\lean{...}` pins + named sub-lemmas).

`\lean{...}` pin mapping (verified):
| Blueprint label | `\lean{}` name | File loc |
|---|---|---|
| `def:coherent_sheaf_flat` | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat` | L208 (def) |
| `thm:generic_flatness` | `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` | L208 (theorem) |
| `thm:flattening_stratification_exists` | `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification` | L358 |
| `thm:flattening_stratification_universal` | `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal` | L399 |

Named sub-lemmas in blueprint's `Mathlib status` section:
- `flatLocusStratification` (L252; matches blueprint identifier)
- `flatLocusReduction` (L280; matches blueprint identifier)
- `flatLocusAssembly` (L310; matches blueprint identifier)
- `flatteningStratification.ofCurve` (L438; matches blueprint
  identifier)

All 4 pinned names match exactly; ready for `sync_leanok` consumption.

## Body-lanes for iter-177+

Recommended ordering (smallest / most-isolable first):

1. **`CoherentSheafFlat` defining lemma**: zero body (it's a `def`). The
   first interface lemmas (e.g. `CoherentSheafFlat.id`,
   `CoherentSheafFlat.comp`, `CoherentSheafFlat.pullback`) are pure
   structural rewrites and could be small extras for iter-177+ if a
   downstream consumer needs them.

2. **`genericFlatness`**: the deepest single body, requires the Nitsure
   §4 algebraic-form proof (Noether normalisation + filtration). Defer
   until Mathlib's `Module.Flat` + `IsAffineOpen.SpecΓIdentity` are
   threaded — likely **multi-iter body lane**.

3. **`flatLocusStratification`**: builds on Nakayama + matrix-ideal
   construction. Mathlib has `Module.Finite.exists_fin_iso` etc.; the
   matrix-entry-ideal locally-closed subscheme construction would need
   new infrastructure. Multi-iter body lane.

4. **`flatLocusReduction`**: consumes `genericFlatness` + Noetherian
   induction on closed complement. Pure Mathlib-Noetherian-induction
   pattern; small body lane *if* `genericFlatness` lands.

5. **`flatLocusAssembly`**: consumes `flatLocusStratification` ×(n+1)
   on direct images `π_*F(N+i)`. Hardest input is the direct-image
   construction `π_*` and the uniform-vanishing-bound `N` — both
   require Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity in Mathlib (gap).

6. **`flatteningStratification`**: pure assembly of 4 + 5 + 7 once
   those land. Small body lane.

7. **`flatteningStratification_universal`**: structural Yoneda
   argument. Small body lane *if* `flatteningStratification` lands.

8. **`flatteningStratification.ofCurve`**: pure base-change + invoke
   `flatteningStratification`. Smallest body lane; could be 1-2 iters
   once main theorem lands.

## Build verification

- `lake build AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.FlatteningStratification` — PASS
  (7 sorry warnings, no errors).
- `lake build AlgebraicJacobian` — PASS (8349 jobs successful; all
  downstream files still build).
- `AlgebraicJacobian.lean` import updated (line 20 inserted after
  `FGAPicRepresentability`).

## Sorry count delta (Lane E contribution)

- Entering iter-176: 0 sorries in this file (file did not exist).
- Exiting iter-176: **+7 sorries** (one per theorem body).

This is the **expected file-skeleton inflation** per
`iter/iter-176/objectives.md` termination table ("E: +~3-6 stubs"). The
+7 is within the upper bound of the prediction.

## Blueprint markers (deferred to review/sync_leanok)

Per project rules, `\leanok` is managed by the deterministic
`sync_leanok` phase between prover and review. This run leaves the
blueprint untouched; `sync_leanok` should mark each pin's statement
block `\leanok` (each declaration is formalised, just with a `sorry`
body) but NOT the proof blocks (all 7 declarations have non-trivial
`sorry`).

## Next iter triage

- **Body lanes (iter-177+)**: prioritise `flatteningStratification.ofCurve`
  + `flatteningStratification_universal` (both small once the main
  theorem lands); the main theorem itself is multi-iter.
- **Infrastructure prerequisites**: relative `ℙⁿ_S`, Castelnuovo–Mumford
  regularity, cohomology-and-base-change Stacks 02KH would unlock the
  main theorem body lane. These are Mathlib-side and not file-local.
- **No reversal triggers fired** — file-skeleton landed cleanly.

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133
