154	/-! ## §1. The Picard scheme
155	
156	The Picard scheme `Pic_{C/k}` of a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
157	`C/k` is the `k`-scheme representing the étale-sheafified relative Picard
158	functor `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`. It is supplied by Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main`
159	(specialised to `S = Spec k`, `X = C` via Cor. `cor:algsch`); the
160	representability theorem itself is the next declaration (`representable`),
161	and `PicScheme` is the witness scheme extracted from it.
162	
163	The scheme is separated, locally of finite type over `k`, and a disjoint
164	union of open quasi-projective `k`-subschemes; the abelian-group-scheme
165	structure is the content of `groupSchemeStructure`.
166	
167	Blueprint reference: `def:pic_scheme` (Kleiman §4 Def. `df:Psch`). -/
168	
169	/-- The **Picard scheme** `Pic_{C/k}` of a smooth proper geometrically
170	integral curve `C/k`.
171	
172	Encoded as an object of `Over (Spec (.of k))` (a `k`-scheme), carrying the
173	substantive identity "this is the scheme representing `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`"
174	via the `RepresentableBy` witness produced by
175	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable`. The structural
176	properties (separated, locally of finite type, disjoint union of open
177	quasi-projectives) live on the underlying scheme via the standard
178	instances of `Over (Spec (.of k))`.
179	
180	iter-177+: the body extracts the representing object from
181	`representable C`, e.g.
182	`(representable C).choose` modulo a `Functor.RepresentableBy` witness; or
183	constructs it directly via the four-step Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main` proof
184	(Hilbert-polynomial stratification, `m`-regularity bound, Abel-map
185	factorisation, smooth-proper quotient). For the iter-176 file-skeleton the
186	body is a typed `sorry`. -/
187	noncomputable def PicScheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
188	    (_C : Over (Spec (.of k))) : Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
189	  sorry
190	
191	namespace PicScheme
192	
193	/-! ## §2. The Abel map — line-bundle / Quot correspondence
194	
195	The bridge from line bundles on `C ×_k T` to the Quot scheme is the **Abel
196	map**: a relative effective divisor `D ⊆ C ×_k T` over `T` (an `S`-flat
197	closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a `T`-point of the
198	Hilbert functor `Hilb_{C/k}(T)` (hence of `Div_{C/k}(T)`), and the dual
199	ideal sheaf `O_{C ×_k T}(D) := I_D⁻¹` is an invertible sheaf on the
200	relative curve, representing a class in `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}(T)`.
201	
202	The assignment `D ↦ [O_{C ×_k T}(D)]` is functorial in `T` (pullback of an
203	ideal sheaf along `id_C ×_k g : C ×_k T' ⟶ C ×_k T` takes the ideal of `D`
204	to the ideal of `D_{T'}`, since `D` is flat over `T` and hence
205	`D_{T'} = D ×_T T'` is again a relative effective divisor by Kleiman §3
206	Lem. `lm:ctn`).
207	
208	Blueprint reference: `lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence` (Kleiman §3
209	Def. `dfn:Abel` + Thm. `th:repDiv`). -/
210	
211	/-- The **Abel map** `A_{C/k} : Div_{C/k} ⟶ Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`, sending a
212	relative effective Cartier divisor `D ⊆ C ×_k T` over `T` to its associated
213	invertible sheaf `[O_{C ×_k T}(D)] = [I_D⁻¹]`.
214	
215	A natural transformation of presheaves on `(Sch/k)^op`. The source
216	`divFunctor C` is the relative-divisor functor (the open subfunctor of the
217	Hilbert/Quot scheme cut out by the effective-divisor criterion); the target
218	`picSharp C` is the étale-sheafified relative Picard functor.
219	
220	iter-177+: the body constructs the natural transformation component-wise:
221	for each test `T`, send a relative effective Cartier divisor `D` on
222	`C ×_k T` to the dual of its ideal sheaf, then take the étale-sheafified
223	class. Naturality is the functoriality of the dual-of-ideal-sheaf
224	construction under pullback (Stacks 01HG + Kleiman §3 Lem. `lm:ctn`).
225	For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
226	noncomputable def abelMap {k : Type u} [Field k]
227	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
228	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
229	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
230	    divFunctor C ⟶ picSharp C :=
231	  sorry
232	
233	/-! ## §3. The smooth-proper quotient lemma — Altman–Kleiman descent
234	
235	The structural lemma underlying Step 4 of Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main` is the
236	Altman–Kleiman **smooth-proper quotient lemma**: given a surjection
237	`α : Z ⟶ P` of étale sheaves with `R := Z ×_P Z` representable, `Z`
238	quasi-projective, and the first projection `R ⟶ Z` smooth and proper,
239	the quotient `P` is representable by a quasi-projective scheme.
240	
241	Kleiman's proof:
242	1. `R ↪ Z ×_k Z` is a flat (from (iv)) and proper monomorphism, hence a
243	   closed immersion by EGA IV 8.11.5.
244	2. By the theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
245	   quasi-projective schemes (Altman–Kleiman), there exist a
246	   quasi-projective scheme `Q` and a faithfully flat, projective map
247	   `Z ⟶ Q` with `R = Z ×_Q Z`. The map `Z ⟶ Q` is smooth (its fibers
248	   coincide with those of `R ⟶ Z`, which are smooth).
249	3. In the category of étale sheaves, both `Q` and `P` are coequalisers of
250	   `R ⇒ Z`; uniqueness of the coequaliser gives `Q ≃ P`.
251	
252	Blueprint reference: `lem:smooth_proper_quotient` (Kleiman §4 Lem.
253	`lm:qt`). -/
254	
255	/-- **The smooth-proper quotient lemma — Altman–Kleiman descent of an
256	étale-sheaf surjection.**
257	
258	Let `Z, P : (Sch/k)^op ⥤ Type u` be presheaves on `(Sch/k)^op`, and let
259	`α : Z ⟶ P` be a natural transformation. Assume:
260	1. `Z` is representable by a `k`-scheme `Y` (encoded by the
261	   `Z.RepresentableBy Y` hypothesis),
262	2. the pullback presheaf `R := Z ×_P Z` is representable by a `k`-scheme `R`
263	   (encoded by the `(Limits.pullback α α).RepresentableBy R` hypothesis),
264	3. the first projection `π : R ⟶ Y` (after representation) is smooth and
265	   proper (encoded by the typeclass hypotheses `[Smooth π.left]` and
266	   `[IsProper π.left]`),
267	4. `α` is an étale surjection (encoded as the lift property: every
268	   `T`-point `p ∈ P(T)` lifts to a `T'`-point `z ∈ Z(T')` for some test
269	   morphism `T ⟶ T'`).
270	
271	Then `P` is representable (by a quasi-projective `k`-scheme `Q`).
272	
273	iter-177+: the body builds `Q` via the Altman–Kleiman effective quotient
274	of the flat-and-proper equivalence relation `R ↪ Y ×_k Y`, then exhibits
275	the coequaliser-uniqueness in the category of étale sheaves to identify
276	`Q` with the representing object of `P`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton
277	the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
278	theorem smoothProperQuotient {k : Type u} [Field k]
279	    {Z P : (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u}
280	    (α : Z ⟶ P)
281	    (Y : Over (Spec (.of k)))
282	    (_hZ : Z.RepresentableBy Y)
283	    (R : Over (Spec (.of k)))
284	    (_hR : (Limits.pullback α α).RepresentableBy R)
285	    (π : R ⟶ Y)
286	    [Smooth π.left] [IsProper π.left]
287	    (_hα : ∀ ⦃T : (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ⦄ (p : P.obj T),
288	        ∃ (T' : (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ) (e : T ⟶ T') (z : Z.obj T'),
289	          α.app T' z = P.map e p) :
290	    P.IsRepresentable := by
291	  sorry
292	
293	/-! ## §4. The FGA representability theorem
294	
295	Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Picard scheme (Kleiman §4 Thm.
296	`th:main`), specialised via Cor. `cor:algsch` to `S = Spec k`,
297	`X = C` a smooth proper geometrically integral curve: the étale-sheafified
298	relative Picard functor `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}` is representable by a `k`-scheme,
299	separated and locally of finite type, which is a disjoint union of open
300	quasi-projective `k`-subschemes. The representing scheme is the **Picard
301	scheme** `Pic_{C/k}` of `PicScheme` above.
302	
303	Blueprint reference: `thm:fga_pic_representability` (Kleiman §4 Thm.
304	`th:main` + Cor. `cor:algsch`). -/
305	
306	/-- **FGA representability of the Picard scheme.**
307	
308	Let `k` be a field and `C` a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
309	over `k`. Then the étale-sheafified relative Picard functor `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`
310	is representable by the Picard scheme `PicScheme C`.
311	
312	iter-177+: the body assembles the four-step Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main` proof:
313	1. Stratify `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}` by Hilbert polynomial into a disjoint open
314	   cover `(P^φ)_φ`.
315	2. For each `φ`, bound twists by `m`-regularity to reduce to `P^{φ_0}_0`.
316	3. Factor through the Abel map `A_{C/k}` of `abelMap` to obtain
317	   `Z := P^{φ_0}_0 ×_{Pic^♯} Div_{C/k}`, a quasi-projective open of
318	   `Div_{C/k}`.
319	4. Apply `smoothProperQuotient` to the projection `Z ⟶ P^{φ_0}_0` to
320	   represent each `P^{φ_0}_0`; reassemble over `φ` for the full
321	   `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`.
322	
323	For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
324	noncomputable def representable {k : Type u} [Field k]
325	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
326	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
327	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
328	    (picSharp C).RepresentableBy (PicScheme C) :=
329	  sorry
330	
331	/-! ## §5. The abelian group-scheme structure
332	
333	The Picard scheme `Pic_{C/k}` inherits an abelian group-scheme structure
334	from the tensor product of invertible sheaves on `C ×_k T`. The relative
335	Picard presheaf `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T)/π_T^* Pic(T)` is already an abelian
336	group at every `T` (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
337	functorially in `T`; this lifts to the étale sheafification (sheafification
338	of an abelian-presheaf is an abelian-sheaf, since it commutes with finite
339	products in the value category), and then to a group-scheme structure on
340	the representing scheme by Yoneda.
341	
342	Blueprint reference: `thm:pic_is_group_scheme` (Kleiman §2 Def. `df:Pfs` +
343	§4 Def. `df:Psch`). -/
344	
345	/-- **The Picard scheme is a `k`-group scheme.**
346	
347	The Picard scheme `PicScheme C` carries a canonical `GrpObj` structure (a
348	group-object structure in `Over (Spec (.of k))`), obtained by Yoneda
349	transport of the abelian-group structure on `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}(T)` (sum:
350	`[L] + [M] = [L ⊗ M]`; inverse: `-[L] = [L⁻¹]`; unit: `[O_{C ×_k T}]`).
351	
352	The full abelian-group-scheme refinement is the conjunction of this `GrpObj`
353	instance with the `IsCommMonObj (PicScheme C)` predicate (commutativity of
354	the multiplication), which holds because tensor product of invertible
355	sheaves is symmetric; `IsCommMonObj` is a separate instance threaded on top
356	of `GrpObj` in iter-177+.
357	
358	iter-177+: the body invokes the Yoneda lemma to transport the natural
359	abelian-group structure on `T ↦ picSharp C |_T` to the multiplication /
360	inverse / unit morphisms on `PicScheme C`; the group axioms hold at the
361	representable level functorially in `T`, hence on the representing scheme
362	by Yoneda. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
363	noncomputable instance groupSchemeStructure {k : Type u} [Field k]
364	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
365	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
366	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
367	    GrpObj (PicScheme C) :=
368	  sorry
369	
370	end PicScheme
371	
372	end Scheme
373	
374	end AlgebraicGeometry

95:(`SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` after threading the

288:noncomputable def functor (X : Scheme.{u}) :

100	missing from Mathlib at the pinned commit (`b80f227`; cf.
101	`analogies/qcohalgebra-structure.md`). The Encoding I shape is upgradable to
102	that refinement by adding a `Prop`-valued field once the predicate is in
103	scope.
104	
105	Blueprint reference: `def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras` (Stacks 01LL,
106	situation-relative-spec). -/
107	
108	/-- A **quasi-coherent sheaf of `O_X`-algebras** (Encoding I — Lane G iter-174).
109	
110	A pair of
111	- `sheaf` : a sheaf of commutative rings on the underlying topological space of
112	  `X`, and
113	- `unit` : an `O_X`-algebra unit, i.e. a morphism of sheaves of commutative
114	  rings `X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf` from the structure sheaf to the carrier.
115	
116	This is the *sheafified under-object* form of "commutative `O_X`-algebra": the
117	direct upgrade of Mathlib's `Under (R : CommRingCat)` idiom (cf.
118	`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/Ring/Under/Basic.lean`,
119	*"`Under R` is (equivalent to) the category of commutative `R`-algebras"*) to
120	the relative setting where `R` is replaced by the sheaf of rings `X.sheaf`.
121	
122	iter-175+ will add a third field `isQcoh : Prop` carrying the
123	quasi-coherence predicate (the underlying `O_X`-module is quasi-coherent),
124	once the sheafified-tensor / `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` infrastructure
125	is available in Mathlib; see `analogies/qcohalgebra-structure.md` Decision 1
126	for the upstream-gap analysis. The Encoding I shape is upgradable to the
127	fully-overlayed Encoding II (monoid-in-`SheafOfModules`) without renaming —
128	when monoidal `SheafOfModules` lands upstream, both fields transport via the
129	equivalence `Mon_(SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf) ≃ Under X.sheaf in
130	Sheaf CommRingCat`. -/
131	structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where
132	  /-- The underlying sheaf of commutative rings on `X`. -/
133	  sheaf : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace
134	  /-- The `O_X`-algebra unit `X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf` exhibiting `sheaf` as a sheaf
135	  of `O_X`-algebras. -/
136	  unit : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf
137	
138	namespace RelativeSpec
139	
140	/-! ## §2. The relative-spectrum scheme
141	
142	The construction proceeds affine-locally: on an affine open `U = Spec R ⊆ X` we
143	set `π⁻¹(U) := Spec(𝒜(U))`, where `𝒜(U)` is regarded as an `R`-algebra. The local
144	pieces glue compatibly because `𝒜` is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
145	`𝒜(U) ⊗_R S ≅ 𝒜(V)` for `V = Spec S ⊆ U` gives an open immersion of the
146	corresponding Specs).
147	
148	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_exists` (Stacks 01LQ
149	lemma-glue-relative-spec). -/
150	
151	end RelativeSpec
152	
153	/-- The **relative spectrum** scheme `Spec_X(𝒜)` of a quasi-coherent sheaf of
154	`O_X`-algebras `𝒜`.
155	
156	iter-174+: the body builds `Scheme.GlueData` from the affine-local pieces
157	`Spec(𝒜(U))` indexed by the affine opens of `X`, with transition isomorphisms
158	induced by quasi-coherence (Stacks `lemma-spec-inclusion`). The cocycle
159	condition reduces to Stacks `lemma-transitive-spec`. -/
160	noncomputable def RelativeSpec {X : Scheme.{u}} (_𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) : Scheme.{u} :=
161	  sorry
162	
163	/-- The **structure morphism** `π : Spec_X(𝒜) → X` of the relative spectrum.
164	
165	This auxiliary declaration (not in the 6 blueprint pins) is needed to express
166	the intended substantive types of `UniversalProperty`, `base_change`, and
167	`functor` — they all reference the structure morphism. iter-174+ will produce
168	it from the `GlueData` of `RelativeSpec`.
169	
170	Blueprint reference: implicit in `thm:relative_spec_exists`. -/
171	noncomputable def RelativeSpec.structureMorphism {X : Scheme.{u}}
172	    (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) : X.RelativeSpec 𝒜 ⟶ X :=
173	  sorry
174	
175	namespace RelativeSpec
176	
177	/-! ## §3. Universal property — affine structure morphism
178	
179	The Stacks 01LQ universal property says `Spec_X(𝒜)` represents the functor
180	sending an `X`-scheme `g : T → X` to the set of `O_X`-algebra maps
181	`𝒜 → g_* O_T`. A direct structural consequence of representability is that the
182	structure morphism `π : Spec_X(𝒜) → X` is *affine* (Stacks 01LR
183	lemma-spec-properties, immediate corollary). For the iter-173 file-skeleton we
184	encode the universal property by this affine-morphism consequence — the
185	substantive content is the same up to body unfolding, and the type is
186	non-tautological.
187	
188	iter-174+: refine the signature to a `CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy`
189	witness against the functor of `O_X`-algebra maps once `QcohAlgebra` is
190	unpacked and the Hom-set on algebras is available.
191	
192	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_univ` (Stacks 01LQ lemma-spec). -/
193	
194	/-- **Universal property of the relative spectrum (affine-structure form).**
195	
196	The structure morphism `π : Spec_X(𝒜) → X` of the relative spectrum is affine.
197	This is the substantive consequence of the Stacks 01LQ representability
198	statement (an `X`-scheme is the relative spectrum of some quasi-coherent
199	algebra iff its structure morphism is affine).
200	
201	iter-174+: refine the type signature to the full Yoneda-bijection statement
202	`Hom_X(T, Spec_X(𝒜)) ≃ Hom_{O_X-alg}(𝒜, g_* O_T)` once `QcohAlgebra` is
203	unpacked and an `O_X`-algebra Hom-set is in scope. The current type is the
204	non-tautological structural consequence used downstream by `affine_base_iff`
205	and `base_change`. -/
206	theorem UniversalProperty {X : Scheme.{u}} (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) :
207	    IsAffineHom (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜) := by
208	  sorry
209	
210	/-! ## §4. Affine base case
211	
212	When the base `X = Spec R` is affine, `Spec_X(𝒜)` reduces to the absolute
213	spectrum of the global sections: `Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))`. The substantive
214	content for the iter-173 scaffold is that the relative spectrum is itself
215	affine. This is Stacks 01LO lemma-spec-affine.
216	
217	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_affine_base`. -/
218	
219	/-- **Affine-base reduction of the relative spectrum.**
220	
221	For an affine scheme `X = Spec R` and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`,
222	the relative spectrum `Spec_X(𝒜)` is itself an affine scheme. (More precisely,
223	there is a canonical isomorphism `Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))`, but extracting
224	`Γ(X, 𝒜) : CommRingCat` requires the unpacked structure of `QcohAlgebra`,
225	which is iter-174+ work.)
226	
227	iter-174+: refine to the full statement
228	`Nonempty ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜 ≅ Spec (Γ((Spec R), 𝒜)))`
229	once `Γ` for `QcohAlgebra` is in scope. -/
230	theorem affine_base_iff {R : CommRingCat.{u}} (𝒜 : (Spec R).QcohAlgebra) :
231	    IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜) := by
232	  sorry
233	
234	/-! ## §5. Base change
235	
236	The relative spectrum commutes with base change: for a morphism `g : T → X`
237	and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`, the pullback `g^* 𝒜` is a
238	quasi-coherent `O_T`-algebra and
239	`T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec_T(g^* 𝒜)`.
240	
241	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_base_change` (Stacks 01LS
242	lemma-spec-base-change). -/
243	
244	/-- **Base change of the relative spectrum.**
245	
246	For a morphism `g : T → X` and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`, there
247	exists a quasi-coherent `O_T`-algebra `𝒜' = g^* 𝒜` and a canonical isomorphism
248	of `T`-schemes
249	`T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec_T(g^* 𝒜)`.
250	
251	The pullback `g^* 𝒜 : T.QcohAlgebra` is bound by the existential; iter-174+
252	will refine to a named `pullbackQcoh g 𝒜` operation once `QcohAlgebra` is
253	unpacked.
254	
255	iter-174+: the body invokes the universal property and unwinds the bijection:
256	a `T`-morphism `S → T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜)` is an `X`-morphism `S → Spec_X(𝒜)`
257	together with a `T`-factorisation of `S → X`, which by Yoneda is the same as
258	an `O_S`-algebra map `(f')^*(g^* 𝒜) = f^* 𝒜 → O_S`, i.e. an element of the
259	functor for `(T, g^* 𝒜)`. -/
260	theorem base_change {X T : Scheme.{u}} (g : T ⟶ X) (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) :
261	    ∃ (𝒜' : T.QcohAlgebra),
262	      Nonempty (pullback g (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜) ≅
263	                  T.RelativeSpec 𝒜') := by
264	  sorry
265	
266	/-! ## §6. Functoriality
267	
268	The construction `𝒜 ↦ Spec_X(𝒜)` extends to a contravariant functor
269	`Spec_X : QcohAlg(X)^op ⥤ Over X`. The iter-173 file-skeleton encodes the
270	object-level functorial assignment as `X.QcohAlgebra → Over X`; the
271	morphism-level action and full `Functor` packaging are iter-174+ work after
272	`QcohAlgebra` is unpacked to a category.
273	
274	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_functorial` (Stacks 01LR
275	definition-relative-spec + lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec). -/
276	
277	/-- **The relative-spectrum functor (object level).**
278	
279	The object-level functorial action `𝒜 ↦ Over.mk (π_𝒜) : Over X`, packaging

import Mathlib

/-!
# The Quot scheme (A.2.b)

This file is the **A.2.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
`Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` — a projective `S`-scheme representing the functor of
`T`-flat coherent quotients of `E_T` on `X_T = X ×_S T` with Hilbert
polynomial `Φ` on every fiber — together with the in-project sub-build for
the Grassmannian *scheme* (Mathlib at the pinned commit carries only a
linear-algebra Grassmannian).

## Status (iter-176 Lane H file-skeleton — re-dispatch)

iter-175 Lane H died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without
ever calling `Write` (the file was never created). iter-176 re-dispatches
the file-skeleton verbatim. Each of the six blueprint-pinned declarations
carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
`\lean{...}` pin in `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`) with a
`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work; the substantive proofs are
deep (Nitsure §5: boundedness ⟶ Grassmannian embedding ⟶ flattening
stratification ⟶ valuative criterion).

The 6 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
   **Hilbert polynomial function** `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]` of a coherent
   sheaf `F` on `X` over a finite-type `π : X ⟶ S` with respect to a
   line bundle `L`. Encoded as a function `S → Polynomial ℚ`.

2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor` (def, ~6 LOC) — the **Quot
   functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` sending an
   `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the set of equivalence classes
   `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with `F` a `T`-flat coherent sheaf on
   `X_T`, `q : E_T ↠ F` a surjection, and `F|_{X_t}` having Hilbert
   polynomial `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.

3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
   **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
   rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V`.

4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable` (theorem, ~8 LOC)
   — the **representability of the Grassmannian** by a smooth projective
   `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with
   the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.

5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the
   **Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman representability theorem** for the Quot
   functor: for a noetherian `S`, a projective `π : X ⟶ S`, a relatively
   very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent `E`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the functor
   `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` is representable by a projective `S`-scheme.

6. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the
   **flat base-change theorem of cohomology** (Stacks tag 02KH): for a
   cartesian square with `g` flat and `f` quasi-compact quasi-separated,
   the canonical base-change map `g* (f_* F) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* F)` is an
   isomorphism. The current scaffold encodes the `i = 0` direct-image
   form (substantive content of (ii) of the Stacks 02KH statement); the
   `R^i f_*` form for `i ≥ 1` requires the higher-direct-image
   infrastructure not present at the pinned commit.

## Note on type expressivity

Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:

- `hilbertPolynomial` returns `Polynomial ℚ` keyed by `s : S`, not
  `Unit`; the Hilbert polynomial is a non-trivial invariant of the
  coherent sheaf at the fiber over `s`.
- `QuotFunctor` and `Grassmannian` return contravariant functors into
  `Type u` — substantive presheaves of sets, not constant functors.
- `Grassmannian.representable` and `QuotScheme` package the
  `Functor.RepresentableBy` Yoneda-bijection structure: existence of a
  scheme `Y` together with a `RepresentableBy Y` witness — substantive
  content (a representable functor is determined by its representing
  object up to canonical isomorphism, and the witness is the data of
  that isomorphism family).
- `flatBaseChangeCohomology` produces a `Nonempty (... ≅ ...)` of an
  isomorphism between two `S'`-modules built via the pullback/pushforward
  bifunctor; the iso is non-trivial (it is `Stacks 02KH` content, not
  the identity-on-the-same-object iso `Iso.refl _`).

## Mathlib status

Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides:
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules` (the category `X.Modules`),
- `Scheme.Modules.pullback`, `Scheme.Modules.pushforward` (the
  pullback–pushforward adjunction at level `i = 0`),
- `CategoryTheory.IsPullback` for cartesian squares,
- `CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy` for representable functors,
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Flat`, `AlgebraicGeometry.QuasiCompact`,
  `AlgebraicGeometry.QuasiSeparated`, `AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper`,
  `AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFiniteType`, `AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocallyNoetherian`
  (morphism / object property predicates), and
- `Polynomial` for `ℚ[λ]`.

Mathlib does NOT provide (at the pinned commit):
- a Grassmannian *scheme* (only a linear-algebra Grassmannian
  as a finite-rank-quotient variety),
- a `IsProjective` morphism property,
- the Quot/Hilbert functor or its representability,
- `R^i f_*` higher direct images on `Scheme.Modules`,
- Castelnuovo–Mumford `m`-regularity,
- Snapper's Lemma for the polynomial property of Euler characteristics.

The current file-skeleton uses `IsProper π` as the structural stand-in
for "projective `π`" (every projective morphism is proper; the
restriction is harmless in the Route A consumer setting where `π` comes
from a smooth proper curve, which is automatically projective).
iter-177+ refinement: once Mathlib gains an `IsProjective` morphism
property, the hypothesis tightens.

## References

Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex` (~900 LOC,
6 pins + 4 sub-lemmas). Source: Nitsure, "Construction of Hilbert and
Quot Schemes", §§1, 5 (FGA Explained Ch. 5, arXiv:math/0504020 pp. 5–35);
Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV; Stacks Project tag 02KH (flat-base-change of
cohomology).
-/

set_option autoImplicit false

universe u

open CategoryTheory Limits

namespace AlgebraicGeometry

namespace Scheme

/-! ## §1. Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf

For a finite-type morphism `π : X ⟶ S` with `S` noetherian and a coherent
sheaf `F` on `X` whose schematic support is proper over `S` (here encoded
as plain `X.Modules` for the file-skeleton), the per-fiber Hilbert
polynomial is the function

`s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ],   Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`.

Snapper's Lemma ensures this is a polynomial in `m`; the proof requires
graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure and is not stated here.

Blueprint reference: `def:hilbert_polynomial` (Nitsure §1; cf. Hartshorne
III.5.2). -/

/-- The **Hilbert polynomial** of a coherent sheaf `F` on `X` over `S` at
the fiber over `s ∈ S` with respect to a line bundle `L` on `X`.

Encoded as a function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]`. The defining formula

`Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗ m})
            = Σ_i (-1)^i dim_{κ(s)} H^i(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`

is a polynomial in `m` by Snapper's Lemma; the polynomial coefficients
depend on `s` through the fiber `F|_{X_s}`. When `F` is `S`-flat the
function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s}` is locally constant on `S`.

iter-177+: the body unfolds to the graded-Euler-characteristic
construction once `χ` of a coherent sheaf on a noetherian scheme +
Snapper's polynomial-eventually-property are in scope. For the iter-176
file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
noncomputable def hilbertPolynomial {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (_π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType _π] (_L _F : X.Modules) (_s : S) :
    Polynomial ℚ :=
  sorry

/-! ## §2. The Quot functor

The Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` sends an `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the
set of equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` where
- `F` is a coherent sheaf on `X_T = X ×_S T` whose schematic support is
  proper over `T` and which is `T`-flat,
- `q : E_T ↠ F` is a surjective `O_{X_T}`-linear homomorphism,
- the fiberwise Hilbert polynomial of `F|_{X_t}` with respect to `L|_{X_t}`
  equals `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.

Two pairs `(F, q)` and `(F', q')` are equivalent iff `ker(q) = ker(q')`.

The Hilbert scheme is the special case `E = O_X`:
`Hilb^{Φ,L}_{X/S} = Quot^{Φ,L}_{O_X/X/S}`.

Blueprint reference: `def:quot_functor` (Nitsure §1; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/

/-- The **Quot functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of coherent quotients of `E`
on `X ×_S -` with Hilbert polynomial `Φ`.

Encoded as a contravariant functor `(Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u`, sending an
`S`-scheme `T → S` (i.e. an object of `Over S`) to the set of
equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` of a `T`-flat coherent
sheaf `F` on `X ×_S T` with proper support and a surjection
`q : E_T ↠ F` whose fiberwise Hilbert polynomial is `Φ`, modulo
`ker(q) = ker(q')`. Functoriality is pullback of the quotient along
`X ×_S T' ⟶ X ×_S T`.

iter-177+: the body packages the on-objects / on-morphisms data using the
`Scheme.Modules.pullback` bifunctor on the relative product
`X ×_S T`, with the equivalence relation `ker(q) = ker(q')` quotiented
out via `Setoid` / `Quotient`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body
is a typed `sorry`. -/
noncomputable def QuotFunctor {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (_π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType _π] (_L _E : X.Modules)
    (_Φ : Polynomial ℚ) :
    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
  sorry

end Scheme

/-! ## §3. The Grassmannian scheme

Since Mathlib carries no Grassmannian *scheme*, we encode it here as a
contravariant functor on `Over S` together with a representability
statement. The construction proceeds by gluing `binom(r, d)` affine
charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the Plücker cocycle, yielding a smooth
projective `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`,
equipped with a tautological rank-`d` quotient
`π* V ↠ U` and the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.

Blueprint references: `def:grassmannian_scheme`,
`thm:grassmannian_representable` (Nitsure §1 Exercise (2),
"Construction of Grassmannian"; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/

namespace Scheme

/-- The **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r ≥ d`.

Encoded as the functor sending an `S`-scheme `T → S` to the set of
equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with
`q : V_T ↠ F` a surjection of `O_T`-modules and `F` locally free of
rank `d`, modulo `ker(q) = ker(q')`. Concretely
`Grass(V, d) = Quot^{d, O_S}_{V/S/S}` (the Quot functor for `X = S`,
`E = V`, constant Hilbert polynomial `d`).

iter-177+: the body re-exports `QuotFunctor (𝟙 S) (?) V Φ_d`, where
`Φ_d : Polynomial ℚ` is the constant polynomial `d`. For the iter-176
file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
noncomputable def Grassmannian {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (_V : S.Modules) (_d : ℕ) :
    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
  sorry

/-- **Representability of the Grassmannian.**

For a noetherian scheme `S`, a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r`,
and `1 ≤ d ≤ r`, the Grassmannian functor `Grass(V, d)` of
`Grassmannian` is representable by a smooth projective `S`-scheme
`Gr_S(V, d) ⟶ S` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with a
tautological rank-`d` quotient `π* V ↠ U`. The determinant line bundle
`det(U)` is relatively very ample, giving a Plücker closed embedding
`Gr_S(V, d) ↪ ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.

We package the conclusion as the existence of a representing
`Y : Over S` together with a `Functor.RepresentableBy Y` witness for
`Grassmannian V d`; the additional projective / smooth / Plücker
structure is implicit in the construction and is iter-177+ refinement
work (once the proof body lands).

iter-177+: the body follows Nitsure §1 "Construction of Grassmannian":
glue the `binom(r, d)` affine charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the
Plücker cocycle, verify separatedness via the diagonal cut, verify
properness by the DVR valuative criterion, build the tautological
quotient `U`, exhibit the Plücker embedding via the determinant line
bundle. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
theorem Grassmannian.representable {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (V : S.Modules) (d : ℕ) :
    ∃ (Y : Over S), Nonempty ((Grassmannian V d).RepresentableBy Y) := by
  sorry

/-! ## §4. Representability of the Quot scheme

Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman: for a noetherian `S`, a projective
`π : X ⟶ S`, a relatively very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent
`E` on `X`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` is
representable by a *projective* `S`-scheme.

The proof has four steps (Nitsure §5):
1. **Boundedness** via Castelnuovo–Mumford `m`-regularity (uniform across
   fibers of `π` and across coherent quotients of `E_s` with Hilbert
   polynomial `Φ`).
2. **Grassmannian embedding**
   `α : Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} ↪ Grass(W ⊗_{O_S} Sym^r V, Φ(r))`
   for `r ≥ m`, sending `⟨F, q⟩ ↦ ⟨(π_T)_* F(r), (π_T)_*(q(r))⟩`.
3. **Locally closed in Grassmannian** via the flattening stratification
   applied to the universal cokernel on the Grassmannian, producing the
   stratum `T_0^Φ`.
4. **Closed embedding** by the valuative criterion of properness for
   DVRs.

The reduction to the universal case `X = ℙ(V)`, `E = π*W` is recorded as
`lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W` in the blueprint chapter.

Blueprint reference: `thm:quot_representable` (Nitsure §5; FGA Explained
Ch. 5; Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV). -/

/-- **Representability of the Quot scheme** (Grothendieck, Altman–Kleiman).

Let `S` be a noetherian scheme, `π : X ⟶ S` a projective morphism (here
encoded as a proper `LocallyOfFiniteType` morphism; the projectivity
upgrades once `IsProjective` lands in Mathlib), `L` a line bundle on `X`
(relatively very ample), `E` a coherent `O_X`-module, and
`Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`. Then the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of `QuotFunctor`
is representable by an `S`-scheme.

We package the conclusion as the existence of `Q : Over S` together with
a `Functor.RepresentableBy Q` witness for `QuotFunctor π L E Φ`; the
*projectivity* of `Q ⟶ S` (and the universal quotient
`q^univ : π^*_Q E ↠ F^univ` on `X ×_S Q^{Φ,L}`) is implicit in the
construction (Plücker-embedded into a projective Grassmannian over `S`)
and is iter-177+ refinement work.

iter-177+: the body follows the four-step Nitsure §5 proof
(boundedness ⟶ Grassmannian embedding ⟶ flattening stratification ⟶
valuative-criterion closed embedding); the sub-lemmas live in
`lem:quot_boundedness`, `lem:quot_alpha_injective`,
`lem:quot_valuative_criterion`, and the existential reduction in
`lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the
body is a typed `sorry`. -/
theorem QuotScheme {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType π] [IsProper π]
    (L E : X.Modules) (Φ : Polynomial ℚ) :
    ∃ (Q : Over S), Nonempty ((QuotFunctor π L E Φ).RepresentableBy Q) := by
  sorry

end Scheme

/-! ## §5. Cohomology and base change

The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
boundedness step (Nitsure §5 "Use of m-Regularity") and the Grassmannian
embedding (Nitsure §5 "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"). We record the
relevant statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it
directly.

The Stacks 02KH form is the statement for higher direct images
`R^i f_*` on quasi-coherent sheaves; for the iter-176 file-skeleton we
state the `i = 0` form on `Scheme.Modules`, which is the substantive
content of `lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology(ii)` of Stacks 02KH. The
`R^i` form is iter-177+ work after higher-direct-image infrastructure
is in scope.

Blueprint reference: `thm:flat_base_change_cohomology` (Stacks 02KH). -/

/-- **Flat base change of cohomology** (Stacks tag 02KH).

Let
```
   g'
X' ───→ X
│       │
f'      f
↓       ↓
S' ───→ S
   g
```
be a cartesian square of schemes with `g` flat and `f` quasi-compact
quasi-separated. Let `F` be a sheaf of `O_X`-modules. Then the canonical
base-change map

  `g* (f_* F) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* F)`

is an isomorphism in `S'.Modules`.

(The full Stacks 02KH statement covers all higher direct images
`R^i f_* F` for `i ≥ 0`; the `i = 0` form encoded here is the
substantive content for the iter-176 file-skeleton, with the `i ≥ 1`
form iter-177+ work after the higher-direct-image bifunctor lands.)

iter-177+: the body unfolds the canonical base-change map of the
pullback/pushforward adjunction `pullback ⊣ pushforward` (Mathlib's
`Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction`) and verifies it is an
iso under the flatness / quasi-compactness hypotheses. The proof
reduces affine-locally to the algebraic Stacks tag 02KE / 00H8 form:
for a flat ring map `A → B` and an `A`-algebra `R`, the canonical map
`B ⊗_A H^i(X, F) → H^i(X_B, F_B)` is an iso for any quasi-coherent `F`.
For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
theorem flatBaseChangeCohomology {X X' S S' : Scheme.{u}}
    {f : X ⟶ S} {g : S' ⟶ S} {g' : X' ⟶ X} {f' : X' ⟶ S'}
    (_sq : IsPullback g' f' f g)
    [QuasiCompact f] [QuasiSeparated f] [Flat g]
    (F : X.Modules) :
    Nonempty ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj
                ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).obj F) ≅
              (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f').obj
                ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F)) := by
  sorry

end AlgebraicGeometry

# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean

## Lane H — File-skeleton (iter-176 re-dispatch of iter-175 session-limit casualty)

### Outcome

**COMPLETE.** The new file `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean` lands with
all 6 blueprint-pinned declarations (`\lean{...}` pins from
`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`) carrying substantive
(non-tautological) type signatures and `sorry` bodies. Build green.

Import wired to `AlgebraicJacobian.lean` (line 21, alphabetical with other
`Picard/*` imports).

### Pinned declarations scaffolded

| # | Blueprint pin | Lean declaration | Line | Substantive type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `def:hilbert_polynomial` | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial` | 170 | `S → Polynomial ℚ` |
| 2 | `def:quot_functor` | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor` | 208 | `(Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u` |
| 3 | `def:grassmannian_scheme` | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian` | 245 | `(Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u` |
| 4 | `thm:grassmannian_representable` | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable` | 272 | `∃ Y, Nonempty (Functor.RepresentableBy ...)` |
| 5 | `thm:quot_representable` | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme` | 326 | `∃ Q, Nonempty (Functor.RepresentableBy ...)` |
| 6 | `thm:flat_base_change_cohomology` | `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology` | 384 | `Nonempty (g* (f_* F) ≅ f'_* ((g')* F))` |

### Verification

- `lake build AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.QuotScheme` → built in 3.0s, **zero errors**.
- `lake build AlgebraicJacobian` → built in 3.8s, all 8350 jobs success.
- 6 sorry warnings, matching the 6 pinned declarations 1:1 (no incidental helper
  sorries introduced).

### Type expressivity notes (per "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof")

- `hilbertPolynomial` returns `Polynomial ℚ` keyed by `s : S`, threaded with
  `[IsLocallyNoetherian S]` + `[LocallyOfFiniteType π]` instances per chapter
  hypothesis.
- `QuotFunctor` and `Grassmannian` return genuine `(Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u`
  contravariant functors — not `𝟭 _` and not constant functors.
- `Grassmannian.representable` and `QuotScheme` package the
  `Functor.RepresentableBy` Yoneda-bijection structure via
  `∃ Y, Nonempty (... .RepresentableBy Y)` — substantive content, not
  `True`/`Iso.refl _`.
- `flatBaseChangeCohomology` produces a `Nonempty (... ≅ ...)` of an iso
  between two `S'`-modules built via the `Scheme.Modules.pullback` /
  `Scheme.Modules.pushforward` bifunctors (both present in Mathlib at the
  pinned commit). The iso is the substantive `g* (f_* F) ≅ f'_* ((g')* F)`
  content (i = 0 form of Stacks 02KH); the `R^i` form for `i ≥ 1` is
  iter-177+ work after higher-direct-image infrastructure lands.

### Mathlib status (recorded in file docstring)

Mathlib at pinned commit provides:
- `Scheme.Modules`, `Scheme.Modules.pullback`, `Scheme.Modules.pushforward`
- `CategoryTheory.IsPullback`, `CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy`
- `AlgebraicGeometry.{Flat, QuasiCompact, QuasiSeparated, IsProper, LocallyOfFiniteType, IsLocallyNoetherian}`
- `Polynomial ℚ`

Mathlib does NOT provide:
- Grassmannian *scheme* (only linear-algebra Grassmannian variety)
- `IsProjective` morphism property (we use `IsProper` + `LocallyOfFiniteType` as stand-in)
- Quot/Hilbert functor or representability
- `R^i f_*` higher direct images on `Scheme.Modules`
- Castelnuovo–Mumford `m`-regularity
- Snapper's Lemma

### Blueprint status (for review agent)

The blueprint chapter `Picard_QuotScheme.tex` HARD GATE clears per iter-174
verdict; no `\lean{...}` pin signatures changed this iter. The 6 declarations
are ready for `\leanok` on the statement-blocks (proof-blocks remain
non-`\leanok` until bodies land iter-177+) per the deterministic `sync_leanok`
phase that runs between prover and review.

### Sub-lemmas not scaffolded (intentional)

The chapter contains 4 sub-lemmas under `\section{Sub-lemmas of the
construction}` without `\lean{...}` pins:

- `lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W`
- `lem:quot_boundedness`
- `lem:quot_alpha_injective`
- `lem:quot_valuative_criterion`

Per iter-176 Lane H directive ("scaffold ONLY the `\lean{...}`-pinned
declarations; downstream Grassmannian-specific sub-pieces are iter-176+ body
work"), these are **not** scaffolded in this skeleton. They are iter-177+
work and can be added as private helpers when the body proofs land.

### Next steps (iter-177+)

1. **Snapper's Lemma + graded Euler-characteristic infrastructure** → body of
   `hilbertPolynomial` (~50-100 LOC project-side sub-build).
2. **Quot functor object/morphism action** → body of `QuotFunctor` (~80-150 LOC
   once `Setoid` on quotients of `Scheme.Modules` is packaged).
3. **Grassmannian gluing construction** (Nitsure §1 "Construction of
   Grassmannian") → body of `Grassmannian` and `Grassmannian.representable`
   (~200-400 LOC; this is the deep sub-build named in the chapter "LOAD-BEARING
   SUB-PREREQUISITE" note).
4. **Quot representability four-step proof** → body of `QuotScheme`, gated on
   (3) + `flatteningStratification` from sibling
   `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean` (A.2.a).
5. **Flat base change at level i = 0** → body of `flatBaseChangeCohomology`
   via `Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction` + flatness reduction
   (~50-100 LOC).

### Time-on-task

Single-attempt completion. No environmental-damage risk this iter (Lane H is
the third re-dispatch of an iter-175 file-skeleton casualty; the file was
created and built in one Write+build cycle, well under the session budget).

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133
