139	/-! ## §1. Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf
140	
141	For a finite-type morphism `π : X ⟶ S` with `S` noetherian and a coherent
142	sheaf `F` on `X` whose schematic support is proper over `S` (here encoded
143	as plain `X.Modules` for the file-skeleton), the per-fiber Hilbert
144	polynomial is the function
145	
146	`s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ],   Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`.
147	
148	Snapper's Lemma ensures this is a polynomial in `m`; the proof requires
149	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure and is not stated here.
150	
151	Blueprint reference: `def:hilbert_polynomial` (Nitsure §1; cf. Hartshorne
152	III.5.2). -/
153	
154	/-- The **Hilbert polynomial** of a coherent sheaf `F` on `X` over `S` at
155	the fiber over `s ∈ S` with respect to a line bundle `L` on `X`.
156	
157	Encoded as a function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]`. The defining formula
158	
159	`Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗ m})
160	            = Σ_i (-1)^i dim_{κ(s)} H^i(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`
161	
162	is a polynomial in `m` by Snapper's Lemma; the polynomial coefficients
163	depend on `s` through the fiber `F|_{X_s}`. When `F` is `S`-flat the
164	function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s}` is locally constant on `S`.
165	
166	iter-177+: the body unfolds to the graded-Euler-characteristic
167	construction once `χ` of a coherent sheaf on a noetherian scheme +
168	Snapper's polynomial-eventually-property are in scope. For the iter-176
169	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
170	noncomputable def hilbertPolynomial {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
171	    (_π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType _π] (_L _F : X.Modules) (_s : S) :
172	    Polynomial ℚ :=
173	  sorry
174	
175	/-! ## §2. The Quot functor
176	
177	The Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` sends an `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the
178	set of equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` where
179	- `F` is a coherent sheaf on `X_T = X ×_S T` whose schematic support is
180	  proper over `T` and which is `T`-flat,
181	- `q : E_T ↠ F` is a surjective `O_{X_T}`-linear homomorphism,
182	- the fiberwise Hilbert polynomial of `F|_{X_t}` with respect to `L|_{X_t}`
183	  equals `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.
184	
185	Two pairs `(F, q)` and `(F', q')` are equivalent iff `ker(q) = ker(q')`.
186	
187	The Hilbert scheme is the special case `E = O_X`:
188	`Hilb^{Φ,L}_{X/S} = Quot^{Φ,L}_{O_X/X/S}`.
189	
190	Blueprint reference: `def:quot_functor` (Nitsure §1; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/
191	
192	/-- The **Quot functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of coherent quotients of `E`
193	on `X ×_S -` with Hilbert polynomial `Φ`.
194	
195	Encoded as a contravariant functor `(Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u`, sending an
196	`S`-scheme `T → S` (i.e. an object of `Over S`) to the set of
197	equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` of a `T`-flat coherent
198	sheaf `F` on `X ×_S T` with proper support and a surjection
199	`q : E_T ↠ F` whose fiberwise Hilbert polynomial is `Φ`, modulo
200	`ker(q) = ker(q')`. Functoriality is pullback of the quotient along
201	`X ×_S T' ⟶ X ×_S T`.
202	
203	iter-177+: the body packages the on-objects / on-morphisms data using the
204	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` bifunctor on the relative product
205	`X ×_S T`, with the equivalence relation `ker(q) = ker(q')` quotiented
206	out via `Setoid` / `Quotient`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body
207	is a typed `sorry`. -/
208	noncomputable def QuotFunctor {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
209	    (_π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType _π] (_L _E : X.Modules)
210	    (_Φ : Polynomial ℚ) :
211	    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
212	  sorry
213	
214	end Scheme
215	
216	/-! ## §3. The Grassmannian scheme
217	
218	Since Mathlib carries no Grassmannian *scheme*, we encode it here as a
219	contravariant functor on `Over S` together with a representability
220	statement. The construction proceeds by gluing `binom(r, d)` affine
221	charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the Plücker cocycle, yielding a smooth
222	projective `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`,
223	equipped with a tautological rank-`d` quotient
224	`π* V ↠ U` and the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
225	
226	Blueprint references: `def:grassmannian_scheme`,
227	`thm:grassmannian_representable` (Nitsure §1 Exercise (2),
228	"Construction of Grassmannian"; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/
229	
230	namespace Scheme
231	
232	/-- The **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
233	rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r ≥ d`.
234	
235	Encoded as the functor sending an `S`-scheme `T → S` to the set of
236	equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with
237	`q : V_T ↠ F` a surjection of `O_T`-modules and `F` locally free of
238	rank `d`, modulo `ker(q) = ker(q')`. Concretely
239	`Grass(V, d) = Quot^{d, O_S}_{V/S/S}` (the Quot functor for `X = S`,
240	`E = V`, constant Hilbert polynomial `d`).
241	
242	iter-177+: the body re-exports `QuotFunctor (𝟙 S) (?) V Φ_d`, where
243	`Φ_d : Polynomial ℚ` is the constant polynomial `d`. For the iter-176
244	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
245	noncomputable def Grassmannian {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
246	    (_V : S.Modules) (_d : ℕ) :
247	    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
248	  sorry
249	
250	/-- **Representability of the Grassmannian.**
251	
252	For a noetherian scheme `S`, a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r`,
253	and `1 ≤ d ≤ r`, the Grassmannian functor `Grass(V, d)` of
254	`Grassmannian` is representable by a smooth projective `S`-scheme
255	`Gr_S(V, d) ⟶ S` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with a
256	tautological rank-`d` quotient `π* V ↠ U`. The determinant line bundle
257	`det(U)` is relatively very ample, giving a Plücker closed embedding
258	`Gr_S(V, d) ↪ ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
259	
260	We package the conclusion as the existence of a representing
261	`Y : Over S` together with a `Functor.RepresentableBy Y` witness for
262	`Grassmannian V d`; the additional projective / smooth / Plücker
263	structure is implicit in the construction and is iter-177+ refinement
264	work (once the proof body lands).
265	
266	iter-177+: the body follows Nitsure §1 "Construction of Grassmannian":
267	glue the `binom(r, d)` affine charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the
268	Plücker cocycle, verify separatedness via the diagonal cut, verify
269	properness by the DVR valuative criterion, build the tautological
270	quotient `U`, exhibit the Plücker embedding via the determinant line
271	bundle. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
272	theorem Grassmannian.representable {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
273	    (V : S.Modules) (d : ℕ) :
274	    ∃ (Y : Over S), Nonempty ((Grassmannian V d).RepresentableBy Y) := by
275	  sorry
276	
277	/-! ## §4. Representability of the Quot scheme
278	
279	Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman: for a noetherian `S`, a projective
280	`π : X ⟶ S`, a relatively very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent
281	`E` on `X`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` is
282	representable by a *projective* `S`-scheme.
283	
284	The proof has four steps (Nitsure §5):
285	1. **Boundedness** via Castelnuovo–Mumford `m`-regularity (uniform across
286	   fibers of `π` and across coherent quotients of `E_s` with Hilbert
287	   polynomial `Φ`).
288	2. **Grassmannian embedding**
289	   `α : Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} ↪ Grass(W ⊗_{O_S} Sym^r V, Φ(r))`
290	   for `r ≥ m`, sending `⟨F, q⟩ ↦ ⟨(π_T)_* F(r), (π_T)_*(q(r))⟩`.
291	3. **Locally closed in Grassmannian** via the flattening stratification
292	   applied to the universal cokernel on the Grassmannian, producing the
293	   stratum `T_0^Φ`.
294	4. **Closed embedding** by the valuative criterion of properness for
295	   DVRs.
296	
297	The reduction to the universal case `X = ℙ(V)`, `E = π*W` is recorded as
298	`lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W` in the blueprint chapter.
299	
300	Blueprint reference: `thm:quot_representable` (Nitsure §5; FGA Explained
301	Ch. 5; Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV). -/
302	
303	/-- **Representability of the Quot scheme** (Grothendieck, Altman–Kleiman).
304	
305	Let `S` be a noetherian scheme, `π : X ⟶ S` a projective morphism (here
306	encoded as a proper `LocallyOfFiniteType` morphism; the projectivity
307	upgrades once `IsProjective` lands in Mathlib), `L` a line bundle on `X`
308	(relatively very ample), `E` a coherent `O_X`-module, and
309	`Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`. Then the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of `QuotFunctor`
310	is representable by an `S`-scheme.
311	
312	We package the conclusion as the existence of `Q : Over S` together with
313	a `Functor.RepresentableBy Q` witness for `QuotFunctor π L E Φ`; the
314	*projectivity* of `Q ⟶ S` (and the universal quotient
315	`q^univ : π^*_Q E ↠ F^univ` on `X ×_S Q^{Φ,L}`) is implicit in the
316	construction (Plücker-embedded into a projective Grassmannian over `S`)
317	and is iter-177+ refinement work.
318	
319	iter-177+: the body follows the four-step Nitsure §5 proof
320	(boundedness ⟶ Grassmannian embedding ⟶ flattening stratification ⟶
321	valuative-criterion closed embedding); the sub-lemmas live in
322	`lem:quot_boundedness`, `lem:quot_alpha_injective`,
323	`lem:quot_valuative_criterion`, and the existential reduction in
324	`lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the
325	body is a typed `sorry`. -/
326	theorem QuotScheme {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
327	    (π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType π] [IsProper π]
328	    (L E : X.Modules) (Φ : Polynomial ℚ) :
329	    ∃ (Q : Over S), Nonempty ((QuotFunctor π L E Φ).RepresentableBy Q) := by
330	  sorry
331	
332	end Scheme
333	
334	/-! ## §5. Cohomology and base change
335	
336	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
337	boundedness step (Nitsure §5 "Use of m-Regularity") and the Grassmannian
338	embedding (Nitsure §5 "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"). We record the
339	relevant statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it
340	directly.
341	
342	The Stacks 02KH form is the statement for higher direct images
343	`R^i f_*` on quasi-coherent sheaves; for the iter-176 file-skeleton we
344	state the `i = 0` form on `Scheme.Modules`, which is the substantive
345	content of `lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology(ii)` of Stacks 02KH. The
346	`R^i` form is iter-177+ work after higher-direct-image infrastructure
347	is in scope.
348	
349	Blueprint reference: `thm:flat_base_change_cohomology` (Stacks 02KH). -/
350	
351	/-- **Flat base change of cohomology** (Stacks tag 02KH).
352	
353	Let
354	```
355	   g'
356	X' ───→ X
357	│       │
358	f'      f
359	↓       ↓
360	S' ───→ S
361	   g
362	```
363	be a cartesian square of schemes with `g` flat and `f` quasi-compact
364	quasi-separated. Let `F` be a sheaf of `O_X`-modules. Then the canonical
365	base-change map
366	
367	  `g* (f_* F) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* F)`
368	
369	is an isomorphism in `S'.Modules`.
370	
371	(The full Stacks 02KH statement covers all higher direct images
372	`R^i f_* F` for `i ≥ 0`; the `i = 0` form encoded here is the
373	substantive content for the iter-176 file-skeleton, with the `i ≥ 1`
374	form iter-177+ work after the higher-direct-image bifunctor lands.)
375	
376	iter-177+: the body unfolds the canonical base-change map of the
377	pullback/pushforward adjunction `pullback ⊣ pushforward` (Mathlib's
378	`Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction`) and verifies it is an
379	iso under the flatness / quasi-compactness hypotheses. The proof
380	reduces affine-locally to the algebraic Stacks tag 02KE / 00H8 form:
381	for a flat ring map `A → B` and an `A`-algebra `R`, the canonical map
382	`B ⊗_A H^i(X, F) → H^i(X_B, F_B)` is an iso for any quasi-coherent `F`.
383	For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
384	theorem flatBaseChangeCohomology {X X' S S' : Scheme.{u}}
385	    {f : X ⟶ S} {g : S' ⟶ S} {g' : X' ⟶ X} {f' : X' ⟶ S'}
386	    (_sq : IsPullback g' f' f g)
387	    [QuasiCompact f] [QuasiSeparated f] [Flat g]
388	    (F : X.Modules) :
389	    Nonempty ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj
390	                ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).obj F) ≅
391	              (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f').obj
392	                ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F)) := by
393	  sorry
394	
395	end AlgebraicGeometry

/-

Authors: Christian Merten
-/
import Mathlib
import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.LineBundlePullback

/-!
# The relative Picard functor and its étale sheafification (A.1.c)

This file is the **A.1.c** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It upgrades the
set-valued relative Picard presheaf
`Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) := Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` of
`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean` (A.1.b) to its
abelian-group-valued refinement, and then to its étale sheafification
`Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`.

## Status (iter-176 Lane G file-skeleton — re-dispatch)

iter-175 Lane G died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window at
06:14 UTC without ever calling `Write` (the file was never created).
iter-176 re-dispatches the file-skeleton verbatim. Each blueprint-pinned
declaration carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
the `\lean{...}` pin in `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex`)
with a `sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work, gated on the A.1.a
`RelativeSpec.lean` and A.1.b `LineBundlePullback.lean` bodies landing
first.

The 5 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup` (instance, ~5 LOC) —
   the **abelian-group instance** on the quotient set
   `Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)` built in A.1.b.
   Mathlib's `QuotientAddGroup` machinery on a normal subgroup of an
   abelian group is the backbone; the project-side work certifies that
   `π_T^*` is a group homomorphism (Stacks 01CR + pullback's
   tensor-product preservation).

2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp` (noncomputable def, ~10 LOC) —
   the **relative Picard presheaf** as a contravariant functor on
   `(Over (Spec k))^op` with values in `AddCommGrpCat`, sending
   `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` with the canonical abelian-group
   structure of (1).

3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial` (noncomputable def,
   ~10 LOC) — the **group-homomorphism strengthening** of the set-valued
   `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` of A.1.b: for `g : T' ⟶ T` over `Spec k`,
   the induced map on quotient sets is in fact an
   `AddMonoidHom`-homomorphism with respect to the structure of (1).

4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf` (noncomputable def,
   ~10 LOC) — the **packaged functor**: bundles the data of (2) and (3)
   into a single `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat` instance.

5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme` (noncomputable def, ~10 LOC) —
   the **étale sheafification** of `PicSharp`. Encoded as
   `presheafToSheaf` applied with `J : GrothendieckTopology` representing
   the étale topology on `Over (Spec k)` (a parameter at this stage
   because Mathlib at the pinned commit does not ship the étale
   Grothendieck topology on schemes; iter-177+ refinement: bind `J` to
   the canonical étale topology once it lands).

Plus one unpinned auxiliary theorem:

6. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.unit` (theorem, ~8 LOC) — the
   **sheafification unit**: a canonical morphism of presheaves
   `PicSharp ⟶ (forget _) ∘ (PicScheme ?).val` that exhibits the
   group-presheaf-to-group-sheaf universal property (statement of
   `thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure` in the blueprint).

## Note on type expressivity

Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:

- `addCommGroup` is an `AddCommGroup` instance on a quotient set whose
  underlying type is itself a typed `sorry` in A.1.b (`OnProduct` carrier);
  the substantive content is the existence of the group structure
  (closure under `+`, inverse, neutral element), which is non-trivial
  even at the level of typed-`sorry` carriers — once the carrier is
  unpacked, the group operations are induced by the tensor product on
  invertible sheaves modulo the subgroup `π_T^* Pic(T)`.

- `PicSharp` returns a contravariant *functor*, not a constant family;
  the object and morphism actions are independently substantive (the
  morphism action is the set-valued `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` of
  A.1.b lifted to a group homomorphism).

- `PicSharp.functorial` returns an `AddMonoidHom`, not a bare set map;
  this is a strict strengthening of the set-valued version in A.1.b.

- `PicSharp.presheaf` returns a `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`,
  which on objects matches `PicSharp` and on morphisms matches
  `PicSharp.functorial` — substantive content is the assembly into a
  single category-theoretic object.

- `PicScheme` returns a `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat` for the given topology
  `J`; the sheafification is non-trivial because `PicSharp` is generally
  not even a Zariski sheaf (Kleiman §2, L1292–L1302).

## Mathlib status

Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides:

- `AddCommGrpCat` (the category of abelian groups, in
  `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Grp.Basic`),
- `CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology` and `CategoryTheory.Sheaf`,
- `CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf` (sheafification functor, in
  `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.ConcreteSheafification` /
  `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.LeftExact`),
- `CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.HasSheafCompose` (for whiskering
  with forgetful functors),
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Etale` (the morphism property; in
  `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Etale`),
- `QuotientAddGroup` (quotient by a normal subgroup).

Mathlib does NOT provide (at the pinned commit):

- a global étale `GrothendieckTopology` on `Over (Spec k)` (only the
  étale morphism property; the Grothendieck topology is `iter-177+`
  upstream / project-side work),
- a representability hookup `PicScheme` ⟹ Picard scheme (handled
  downstream in `chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`).

The file-skeleton takes `J : GrothendieckTopology (Over (Spec k))` as
an explicit parameter on `PicScheme` (and on `PicScheme.unit`) so the
declaration is well-typed at the pinned commit. iter-177+: specialise
`J` to the canonical étale Grothendieck topology when it lands.

## References

Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex` (522 LOC,
5 pins). Source: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained
Ch.9 §9.2), Definitions `df:aPf` (absolute Picard functor) +
`df:Pfs` (relative Picard functor, including the étale-sheafified form
`Pic_{(X/S)ét}`); Stacks Project tag 01CR (abelian-group structure on
the Picard group via tensor product).
-/

set_option autoImplicit false

universe u

open CategoryTheory Limits

namespace AlgebraicGeometry

namespace Scheme

namespace PicSharp

/-! ## §1. Abelian-group structure on the relative Picard quotient

The Picard group `Pic(X)` of any scheme is canonically an abelian group
under tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of
`[L]` is `[L⁻¹] = [Hom_{O_X}(L, O_X)]`). The pullback map
`π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_k T)` of
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection` (A.1.b)
respects this structure: it sends `O_T ↦ O_{C ×_k T}` (the structure
sheaf is preserved by inverse image) and is multiplicative on tensor
products (Stacks 01HH for invertibility, Mathlib's
`Scheme.Modules.pullback` for the underlying multiplicativity).

Therefore `π_T^* Pic(T)` is a subgroup of `Pic(C ×_k T)`, and the
quotient `Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` inherits a canonical
abelian-group structure under which the quotient map is a surjective
homomorphism with kernel exactly `π_T^* Pic(T)`.

Blueprint reference: `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid` (Kleiman §2,
Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`; Stacks tag 01CR). -/

/-- **Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient.**

For a base scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, and a test
object `πT : T ⟶ S`, the quotient set
```
Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)
  = Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
```
carries a canonical abelian-group structure: addition is the descent of
tensor product `[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']`, the zero element is the class
`[O_{C ×_S T}]`, and the inverse is `-[L] := [L⁻¹]` (the dual line
bundle). Well-definedness on the quotient uses the subgroup property of
`π_T^* Pic(T)` from `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid`.

iter-177+: the body builds the `AddCommGroup` instance via
Mathlib's `QuotientAddGroup` on the abelian group `Pic(C ×_S T)` (tensor
product) modulo the subgroup `π_T^* Pic(T)` (image of a group hom).
For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry` because the
underlying carrier `LineBundle.OnProduct` is itself a typed `sorry` in
A.1.b (so no concrete tensor-product law is yet available); the
signature still asserts the substantive group instance on the named
quotient set. -/
noncomputable instance addCommGroup {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) :
    AddCommGroup (Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)) :=
  sorry

end PicSharp

/-! ## §2. The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued functor

We assemble the data of A.1.b (object-level quotient set,
`RelPicPresheaf.functorial` morphism action) and §1 (abelian-group
instance on each quotient) into a single contravariant functor

```
PicSharp_{C/k} : (Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat
```

sending an `Spec k`-scheme `T` to the abelian group
`Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` (with the structure of §1), and a
morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `Spec k` to the group homomorphism
`g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) → Pic^♯_{C/k}(T')` descended from the
line-bundle pullback `g_C^* := (id_C ×_k g)^*`.

Blueprint reference: `def:rel_pic_sharp` (Kleiman §2, Def. `df:Pfs`). -/

/-- The **relative Picard functor** of a smooth proper geometrically
integral curve `C` over a field `k`, as a contravariant functor

```
PicSharp_{C/k} : (Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat
```

On objects: `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`, the abelian-group
quotient of `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid` (Lean instance
`PicSharp.addCommGroup`).

On morphisms: `g ↦ g^♯`, the group homomorphism descended from
`g_C^* = (id_C ×_k g)^*` via the quotient (the set-level map is
`RelPicPresheaf.functorial`; the group-hom upgrade is
`PicSharp.functorial` below).

Universe: object map values are `AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}` because the
underlying carrier `Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` lives in
`Type (u+1)` (since `LineBundle.OnProduct` is defined to land in
`Type (u+1)`).

iter-177+: the body builds the functor explicitly from
`AddCommGrpCat.of (Quotient (preimage_subgroup C.hom T.hom))` on
objects and from `PicSharp.functorial` on morphisms. The identity and
composition laws follow from `Scheme.Modules.pullbackId` /
`Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp` descended through the quotient. For the
iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
noncomputable def PicSharp {k : Type u} [Field k] (_C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 _C.hom] [IsProper _C.hom] :
    (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} :=
  sorry

namespace PicSharp

/-! ## §3. Functoriality (group-homomorphism strengthening)

The naturality lemma `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` of A.1.b produces, for
each morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, a set map
```
g^♯ : Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T) ⟶ Pic(C ×_S T') / π_{T'}^* Pic(T')
```
of quotient sets. Combined with the abelian-group instance of §1 on
both sides, this set map upgrades to an additive-monoid homomorphism
`AddMonoidHom`: indeed `g_C^*` preserves tensor products and the
structure sheaf, so it preserves the abelian-group operations on both
sides; the upgrade is the substantive content of
`lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial`.

Blueprint reference: `lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial` (Kleiman §2,
Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`). -/

/-- **Functoriality of the relative Picard presheaf, group-hom form.**

For a base scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, test objects
`πT : T ⟶ S` and `πT' : T' ⟶ S`, and a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`
(encoded by `πT' = g ≫ πT`), the set map
`RelPicPresheaf.functorial πC πT πT' g hg` upgrades to an
`AddMonoidHom`-homomorphism with respect to the abelian-group structure
of `PicSharp.addCommGroup` on source and target.

iter-177+: the body builds the `AddMonoidHom` from the set map by
verifying `map_zero` (the image of `[O_{C ×_S T}]` is `[O_{C ×_S T'}]`,
i.e. pullback of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf) and
`map_add` (pullback preserves tensor products: Stacks 01HG /
`Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensor`). For the iter-176 file-skeleton the
body is a typed `sorry`. -/
noncomputable def functorial {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T)
    (_hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) :
    Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT) →+
      Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT') :=
  sorry

/-! ## §4. Wrapping as a functor instance

The group-valued presheaf bundling: combine the on-objects assignment
of `PicSharp` (carrying the `addCommGroup` structure) with the
on-morphisms assignment of `functorial` (each a group hom) into a
single functor `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`. The identity /
composition laws of the functor are inherited from the corresponding
identities on `Scheme.Modules.pullback`, descended through the
quotient.

This wrapper is kept distinct from `PicSharp` to mirror the blueprint
split (`def:rel_pic_sharp` vs.\ `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf`) and to
record the explicit re-packaging step from the lemma-by-lemma data
(`addCommGroup`, `PicSharp`, `functorial`) into a single
category-theoretic functor — useful when applying functorial
constructions (Yoneda, sheafification, …) to the *bundled* form.

Blueprint reference: `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf` (Kleiman §2,
Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`). -/

/-- **The relative Picard presheaf, bundled.**

The relative Picard functor `PicSharp_{C/k}` packaged as a single
contravariant functor `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`, with object
action `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` (group structure
`PicSharp.addCommGroup`) and morphism action `g ↦ g^♯` (group hom from
`PicSharp.functorial`). Identity and composition laws are inherited
from `Scheme.Modules.pullbackId` / `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp`
descended through the quotient.

iter-177+: the body either re-exports `PicSharp` (if the
`AddCommGrpCat`-valued functor is built directly in §2) or constructs
the functor from the on-objects/on-morphisms data using the
`CategoryTheory.Functor.mk` builder; the body is a typed `sorry` for
the iter-176 file-skeleton.

Note on naming: this is the canonical "fully assembled" form mentioned
as `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf` in the blueprint; it is structurally a
`def` (not a `theorem`) because the conclusion is a `Functor` (data),
not a `Prop`. -/
noncomputable def presheaf {k : Type u} [Field k] (_C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 _C.hom] [IsProper _C.hom] :
    (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} :=
  sorry

end PicSharp

/-! ## §5. Étale sheafification

The relative Picard presheaf `PicSharp_{C/k}` is generally not even a
Zariski sheaf (Kleiman §2 L1292–L1302). To obtain a representable
functor in the sense of `chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`
(downstream A.2.c), one replaces `PicSharp_{C/k}` by its **associated
étale sheaf** `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét} := (PicSharp_{C/k})^{∼ét}`. Kleiman §4
(Theorem `th:main`) represents precisely this sheafified functor.

For the iter-176 file-skeleton, we encode the sheafification as
`presheafToSheaf J _` applied to the bundled `PicSharp.presheaf` for a
**parameter** Grothendieck topology `J` on `Over (Spec k)` — because
Mathlib at the pinned commit does not ship an étale Grothendieck
topology on schemes (it ships only the morphism property
`AlgebraicGeometry.Etale`). iter-177+ refinement: bind `J` to the
canonical étale topology once it lands in Mathlib.

Blueprint reference: `def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification` (Kleiman §2,
`df:Pfs` étale-sheaf clause). -/

/-- The **étale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf**.

Given a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C` over a field
`k`, and a Grothendieck topology `J` on `Over (Spec k)` (intended: the
canonical étale topology on `(Sch/k)`), the **étale-sheafified relative
Picard functor**

```
Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét} := (PicSharp_{C/k})^{~_ét}
```

is the sheafification of the group-valued presheaf
`PicSharp.presheaf` of `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf`. Equivalently, it is
the unique (up to canonical isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the
site `(Over (Spec k), J)` equipped with a presheaf morphism
`PicSharp.presheaf ⟶ (forget _) ∘ -` universal among presheaf morphisms
to abelian-group sheaves.

Encoded as a `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}` object; iter-177+ implementations
will fix `J` to the étale topology and unfold via
`CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf` (Mathlib's
`Sites.ConcreteSheafification`). The body of the iter-176 file-skeleton
is a typed `sorry`.

In Kleiman's notation with `X = C` and `S = Spec k`, this is
`Pic_{(X/S)ét}` from `df:Pfs`. -/
noncomputable def PicScheme {k : Type u} [Field k] (_C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 _C.hom] [IsProper _C.hom]
    (J : GrothendieckTopology (Over (Spec (.of k)))) :
    Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} :=
  sorry

namespace PicScheme

/-! ## §6. Sheafification preserves the abelian-group structure

The étale sheafification of an abelian-group-valued presheaf is itself
abelian-group-valued: the forgetful functor `AddCommGrpCat ⥤ Type`
preserves filtered colimits (these are computed pointwise), and the
plus-construction is built from filtered colimits, so sheafification
commutes with the forgetful functor up to canonical isomorphism. The
substantive content is the **sheafification unit**: a canonical
morphism of presheaves `PicSharp.presheaf ⟶ PicScheme.val` (in
`AddCommGrpCat`) that exhibits the universal property of sheafification.

This statement does NOT have an explicit `\lean{...}` pin in the
blueprint (`thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure` is a description
rather than a Lean target); we surface it here so the downstream
representability statement (`chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`) can
cite the unit directly.

Blueprint reference: `thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure` (Kleiman
§2, `df:Pfs`; standard `Sheafification.toSheafify` content). -/

/-- **Sheafification unit for the étale Picard sheafification.**

Given the étale-sheafified relative Picard functor
`PicScheme C J : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`, there is a canonical morphism
of (group-valued) presheaves
```
η_C : PicSharp.presheaf C ⟶ (PicScheme C J).val
```
in the functor category `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`. This
morphism is the **unit of sheafification**: it is universal among
morphisms from `PicSharp.presheaf C` to any abelian-group-valued
étale sheaf.

iter-177+: the body returns
`(CategoryTheory.toSheafify J (PicSharp.presheaf C))` (using Mathlib's
`Sites.ConcreteSheafification` API) — assuming `J` admits abelian-group
sheafification, which is provided by `HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat`
(supplied by Mathlib for the relevant universes via its
abelian-category-target sheafification). For the iter-176 file-skeleton
the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
theorem unit {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
    (J : GrothendieckTopology (Over (Spec (.of k)))) :
    Nonempty (PicSharp.presheaf C ⟶ (PicScheme C J).val) := by
  sorry

end PicScheme

end Scheme

end AlgebraicGeometry

236	Altman–Kleiman **smooth-proper quotient lemma**: given a surjection
237	`α : Z ⟶ P` of étale sheaves with `R := Z ×_P Z` representable, `Z`
238	quasi-projective, and the first projection `R ⟶ Z` smooth and proper,
239	the quotient `P` is representable by a quasi-projective scheme.
240	
241	Kleiman's proof:
242	1. `R ↪ Z ×_k Z` is a flat (from (iv)) and proper monomorphism, hence a
243	   closed immersion by EGA IV 8.11.5.
244	2. By the theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
245	   quasi-projective schemes (Altman–Kleiman), there exist a
246	   quasi-projective scheme `Q` and a faithfully flat, projective map
247	   `Z ⟶ Q` with `R = Z ×_Q Z`. The map `Z ⟶ Q` is smooth (its fibers
248	   coincide with those of `R ⟶ Z`, which are smooth).
249	3. In the category of étale sheaves, both `Q` and `P` are coequalisers of
250	   `R ⇒ Z`; uniqueness of the coequaliser gives `Q ≃ P`.
251	
252	Blueprint reference: `lem:smooth_proper_quotient` (Kleiman §4 Lem.
253	`lm:qt`). -/

255	/-- **The smooth-proper quotient lemma — Altman–Kleiman descent of an
256	étale-sheaf surjection.**
257	
258	Let `Z, P : (Sch/k)^op ⥤ Type u` be presheaves on `(Sch/k)^op`, and let
259	`α : Z ⟶ P` be a natural transformation. Assume:
260	1. `Z` is representable by a `k`-scheme `Y` (encoded by the
261	   `Z.RepresentableBy Y` hypothesis),
262	2. the pullback presheaf `R := Z ×_P Z` is representable by a `k`-scheme `R`
263	   (encoded by the `(Limits.pullback α α).RepresentableBy R` hypothesis),
264	3. the first projection `π : R ⟶ Y` (after representation) is smooth and
265	   proper (encoded by the typeclass hypotheses `[Smooth π.left]` and
266	   `[IsProper π.left]`),
267	4. `α` is an étale surjection (encoded as the lift property: every
268	   `T`-point `p ∈ P(T)` lifts to a `T'`-point `z ∈ Z(T')` for some test
269	   morphism `T ⟶ T'`).
270	
271	Then `P` is representable (by a quasi-projective `k`-scheme `Q`).
272

276	`Q` with the representing object of `P`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton

never calling `Write`. iter-176 re-dispatched. The file now exists at
`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean` (451 LOC) with 6 `sorry`-bodied
declarations:

| # | Name | Kind | Line | Blueprint pin |
|---|------|------|------|---------------|
| 1 | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup` | instance | 205 | `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid` |
| 2 | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp` | noncomputable def | 257 | `def:rel_pic_sharp` |
| 3 | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial` | noncomputable def | 296 | `lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial` |
| 4 | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf` | noncomputable def | 343 | `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf` |
| 5 | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf` | noncomputable def | 402 | `def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification` (renamed; see below) |
| 6 | `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheafUnit` | theorem | 451 | unpinned (`thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure`) |

Import added to `AlgebraicJacobian.lean`:

```
import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.RelPicFunctor
```

`lake build AlgebraicJacobian` is green; 6 expected `sorry`-warnings on this
file; no errors anywhere.

## Naming conflict flagged (NEEDS BLUEPRINT UPDATE)

The blueprint chapter `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex` pins
`def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification` (line 303) and the §"Lean encoding"
bullet (line 442) under

```
\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
```

but **that name was already on disk** in
`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean:187` for an
unrelated declaration:

```
noncomputable def PicScheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
    (_C : Over (Spec (.of k))) : Over (Spec (.of k)) := sorry
```

The two are different mathematical objects: A.1.c's `PicScheme` is the
*étale sheafification* (a `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`), while A.2.c's
`PicScheme` is the *representing scheme* (an `Over (Spec k)`). Adding
A.1.c verbatim under the blueprint's pin causes a `lake build` failure:

```
import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.FGAPicRepresentability failed,
  environment already contains 'AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme' from
  AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.RelPicFunctor
```

**Mitigation applied (this iter)**: I renamed the A.1.c declaration to
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf` and the auxiliary theorem to

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2

4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

9	# Relative spectrum of a quasi-coherent sheaf of algebras (A.1.a)
10	
11	This file is the **A.1.a** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the relative-spectrum
13	functor `Spec_X(𝒜) : QcohAlg(X)^op ⥤ Sch/X` used by the relative Picard functor on a
14	product `C ×_k T`.
15	
16	## Status (iter-174 Lane G — `QcohAlgebra` carrier landed)
17	
18	iter-173 Lane B scaffolded the six pinned declarations with `sorry` bodies and
19	a type-level `sorry` on `QcohAlgebra`. **iter-174 Lane G** replaces the
20	type-level `sorry` on `QcohAlgebra` with a concrete **Encoding I** structure
21	(sheafified `Under`-object form: `sheaf : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat X` plus a
22	unit morphism from the structure sheaf); see
23	`analogies/qcohalgebra-structure.md` for the upstream-gap analysis on the
24	quasi-coherence overlay (`SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` requires the
25	sheafified-tensor infrastructure that is missing from Mathlib at the pinned
26	commit). The remaining 5 sorry bodies (`RelativeSpec`,
27	`RelativeSpec.structureMorphism`, `UniversalProperty`, `affine_base_iff`,
28	`base_change`) now typecheck against the concrete carrier and remain iter-175+
29	work, gated on the sibling chapters `A.1.b` (`Picard_LineBundlePullback.tex`)
30	and `A.1.c` (relative Picard functor).
31	
32	The 6 pinned declarations are:
33	
34	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra` (**structure**, Encoding I — Lane G
35	   iter-174) — a sheaf of commutative rings on `X` together with an
36	   `O_X`-algebra unit `X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`. The quasi-coherence overlay is
37	   iter-175+ work; see `analogies/qcohalgebra-structure.md` Decision 1.
38	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec` (noncomputable def, ~10 LOC)
39	   — the relative-spectrum scheme.
40	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty` (theorem, ~15 LOC)
41	   — the structure morphism `Spec_X(𝒜) → X` is an affine morphism; this is the
42	     substantive consequence of the representability statement of Stacks 01LQ.
43	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff` (theorem, ~8 LOC)
44	   — when `X = Spec R` the relative spectrum is affine (Stacks 01LO).
45	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.base_change` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
46	   — `RelativeSpec` commutes with base change (Stacks 01LS).
47	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.functor` (def, ~8 LOC)
48	   — the object-level functorial assignment `QcohAlg(X) → Over X`.
49	
50	## Note on type expressivity
51	
52	With Lane G landed, `QcohAlgebra X` carries a non-tautological structure
53	(sheaf-of-CommRings + unit). The remaining 5 sorry bodies still encode each
54	theorem by its *intended substantive consequence* (e.g. the universal property
55	is encoded as "the structure morphism is affine", which the representability
56	statement of Stacks 01LQ structurally implies; base change is encoded as an
57	existential on the pulled-back algebra). Following the project rule "Never
58	weaken the type to dodge the proof", the litmus test for each declaration is
59	that unfolding it reveals a non-tautological claim, not `Iso.refl _` or
60	`trivial`. iter-175+ will refine `UniversalProperty` to a
61	`CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy` witness once the
62	`O_X`-algebra Hom-set is wired up via the under-category form
63	`Under X.sheaf ⊆ TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat X`.

83	/-! ## §1. Quasi-coherent sheaves of `O_X`-algebras
84	
85	For a scheme `X`, a quasi-coherent sheaf of `O_X`-algebras is a sheaf of
86	`O_X`-algebras whose underlying `O_X`-module is quasi-coherent. Stacks tag
87	01LL packages the notion as a sheaf $\mathcal{A}$ on $X$ taking values in
88	commutative rings together with a unit map from the structure sheaf
89	$\mathcal{O}_X$; equivalently, an object of the under-category
90	$\mathcal{O}_X / \operatorname{Sh}(X, \mathbf{CommRing})$.
91	
92	iter-174 (Lane G) instantiates this as **Encoding I** (sheafified
93	`Under`-object form): a pair of a sheaf of commutative rings and a unit
94	morphism from the structure sheaf. The quasi-coherence overlay
95	(`SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` after threading the
96	`HasSheafCompose`/`HasWeakSheafify`/`WEqualsLocallyBijective` instances) is
97	iter-175+ work — it is the *refinement* of "sheaf of `O_X`-algebras" to
98	"quasi-coherent sheaf of `O_X`-algebras" and requires the sheafified
99	tensor-product infrastructure on `SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf`, which is
100	missing from Mathlib at the pinned commit (`b80f227`; cf.
101	`analogies/qcohalgebra-structure.md`). The Encoding I shape is upgradable to
102	that refinement by adding a `Prop`-valued field once the predicate is in
103	scope.
104	
105	Blueprint reference: `def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras` (Stacks 01LL,
106	situation-relative-spec). -/
107	
108	/-- A **quasi-coherent sheaf of `O_X`-algebras** (Encoding I — Lane G iter-174).
109	
110	A pair of
111	- `sheaf` : a sheaf of commutative rings on the underlying topological space of
112	  `X`, and
113	- `unit` : an `O_X`-algebra unit, i.e. a morphism of sheaves of commutative
114	  rings `X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf` from the structure sheaf to the carrier.
115	
116	This is the *sheafified under-object* form of "commutative `O_X`-algebra": the
117	direct upgrade of Mathlib's `Under (R : CommRingCat)` idiom (cf.
118	`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/Ring/Under/Basic.lean`,
119	*"`Under R` is (equivalent to) the category of commutative `R`-algebras"*) to
120	the relative setting where `R` is replaced by the sheaf of rings `X.sheaf`.
121	
122	iter-175+ will add a third field `isQcoh : Prop` carrying the
123	quasi-coherence predicate (the underlying `O_X`-module is quasi-coherent),
124	once the sheafified-tensor / `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` infrastructure
125	is available in Mathlib; see `analogies/qcohalgebra-structure.md` Decision 1
126	for the upstream-gap analysis. The Encoding I shape is upgradable to the
127	fully-overlayed Encoding II (monoid-in-`SheafOfModules`) without renaming —
128	when monoidal `SheafOfModules` lands upstream, both fields transport via the
129	equivalence `Mon_(SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf) ≃ Under X.sheaf in
130	Sheaf CommRingCat`. -/
131	structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where
132	  /-- The underlying sheaf of commutative rings on `X`. -/
133	  sheaf : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace
134	  /-- The `O_X`-algebra unit `X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf` exhibiting `sheaf` as a sheaf
135	  of `O_X`-algebras. -/
136	  unit : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf
137	
138	namespace RelativeSpec
139	
140	/-! ## §2. The relative-spectrum scheme
141	
142	The construction proceeds affine-locally: on an affine open `U = Spec R ⊆ X` we
143	set `π⁻¹(U) := Spec(𝒜(U))`, where `𝒜(U)` is regarded as an `R`-algebra. The local
144	pieces glue compatibly because `𝒜` is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
145	`𝒜(U) ⊗_R S ≅ 𝒜(V)` for `V = Spec S ⊆ U` gives an open immersion of the
146	corresponding Specs).
147	
148	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_exists` (Stacks 01LQ
149	lemma-glue-relative-spec). -/
150	
151	end RelativeSpec
152	
153	/-- The **relative spectrum** scheme `Spec_X(𝒜)` of a quasi-coherent sheaf of
154	`O_X`-algebras `𝒜`.
155	
156	iter-174+: the body builds `Scheme.GlueData` from the affine-local pieces
157	`Spec(𝒜(U))` indexed by the affine opens of `X`, with transition isomorphisms
158	induced by quasi-coherence (Stacks `lemma-spec-inclusion`). The cocycle
159	condition reduces to Stacks `lemma-transitive-spec`. -/
160	noncomputable def RelativeSpec {X : Scheme.{u}} (_𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) : Scheme.{u} :=
161	  sorry
162	
163	/-- The **structure morphism** `π : Spec_X(𝒜) → X` of the relative spectrum.
164	
165	This auxiliary declaration (not in the 6 blueprint pins) is needed to express
166	the intended substantive types of `UniversalProperty`, `base_change`, and
167	`functor` — they all reference the structure morphism. iter-174+ will produce
168	it from the `GlueData` of `RelativeSpec`.
169	
170	Blueprint reference: implicit in `thm:relative_spec_exists`. -/
171	noncomputable def RelativeSpec.structureMorphism {X : Scheme.{u}}
172	    (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) : X.RelativeSpec 𝒜 ⟶ X :=
173	  sorry
174	
175	namespace RelativeSpec
176	
177	/-! ## §3. Universal property — affine structure morphism
178	
179	The Stacks 01LQ universal property says `Spec_X(𝒜)` represents the functor
180	sending an `X`-scheme `g : T → X` to the set of `O_X`-algebra maps
181	`𝒜 → g_* O_T`. A direct structural consequence of representability is that the
182	structure morphism `π : Spec_X(𝒜) → X` is *affine* (Stacks 01LR
183	lemma-spec-properties, immediate corollary). For the iter-173 file-skeleton we
184	encode the universal property by this affine-morphism consequence — the
185	substantive content is the same up to body unfolding, and the type is
186	non-tautological.
187	
188	iter-174+: refine the signature to a `CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy`
189	witness against the functor of `O_X`-algebra maps once `QcohAlgebra` is
190	unpacked and the Hom-set on algebras is available.
191	
192	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_univ` (Stacks 01LQ lemma-spec). -/
193	
194	/-- **Universal property of the relative spectrum (affine-structure form).**
195	
196	The structure morphism `π : Spec_X(𝒜) → X` of the relative spectrum is affine.
197	This is the substantive consequence of the Stacks 01LQ representability
198	statement (an `X`-scheme is the relative spectrum of some quasi-coherent
199	algebra iff its structure morphism is affine).
200	
201	iter-174+: refine the type signature to the full Yoneda-bijection statement
202	`Hom_X(T, Spec_X(𝒜)) ≃ Hom_{O_X-alg}(𝒜, g_* O_T)` once `QcohAlgebra` is
203	unpacked and an `O_X`-algebra Hom-set is in scope. The current type is the
204	non-tautological structural consequence used downstream by `affine_base_iff`
205	and `base_change`. -/
206	theorem UniversalProperty {X : Scheme.{u}} (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) :
207	    IsAffineHom (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜) := by
208	  sorry
209	
210	/-! ## §4. Affine base case
211	
212	When the base `X = Spec R` is affine, `Spec_X(𝒜)` reduces to the absolute
213	spectrum of the global sections: `Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))`. The substantive
214	content for the iter-173 scaffold is that the relative spectrum is itself
215	affine. This is Stacks 01LO lemma-spec-affine.
216	
217	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_affine_base`. -/
218	
219	/-- **Affine-base reduction of the relative spectrum.**
220	
221	For an affine scheme `X = Spec R` and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`,
222	the relative spectrum `Spec_X(𝒜)` is itself an affine scheme. (More precisely,
223	there is a canonical isomorphism `Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))`, but extracting
224	`Γ(X, 𝒜) : CommRingCat` requires the unpacked structure of `QcohAlgebra`,
225	which is iter-174+ work.)
226	
227	iter-174+: refine to the full statement
228	`Nonempty ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜 ≅ Spec (Γ((Spec R), 𝒜)))`
229	once `Γ` for `QcohAlgebra` is in scope. -/
230	theorem affine_base_iff {R : CommRingCat.{u}} (𝒜 : (Spec R).QcohAlgebra) :
231	    IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜) := by
232	  sorry
233	
234	/-! ## §5. Base change
235	
236	The relative spectrum commutes with base change: for a morphism `g : T → X`
237	and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`, the pullback `g^* 𝒜` is a
238	quasi-coherent `O_T`-algebra and
239	`T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec_T(g^* 𝒜)`.
240	
241	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_base_change` (Stacks 01LS
242	lemma-spec-base-change). -/
243	
244	/-- **Base change of the relative spectrum.**
245	
246	For a morphism `g : T → X` and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`, there
247	exists a quasi-coherent `O_T`-algebra `𝒜' = g^* 𝒜` and a canonical isomorphism
248	of `T`-schemes
249	`T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec_T(g^* 𝒜)`.
250	
251	The pullback `g^* 𝒜 : T.QcohAlgebra` is bound by the existential; iter-174+
252	will refine to a named `pullbackQcoh g 𝒜` operation once `QcohAlgebra` is
253	unpacked.
254	
255	iter-174+: the body invokes the universal property and unwinds the bijection:
256	a `T`-morphism `S → T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜)` is an `X`-morphism `S → Spec_X(𝒜)`
257	together with a `T`-factorisation of `S → X`, which by Yoneda is the same as
258	an `O_S`-algebra map `(f')^*(g^* 𝒜) = f^* 𝒜 → O_S`, i.e. an element of the
259	functor for `(T, g^* 𝒜)`. -/
260	theorem base_change {X T : Scheme.{u}} (g : T ⟶ X) (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) :
261	    ∃ (𝒜' : T.QcohAlgebra),
262	      Nonempty (pullback g (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜) ≅
263	                  T.RelativeSpec 𝒜') := by
264	  sorry
265	
266	/-! ## §6. Functoriality
267	
268	The construction `𝒜 ↦ Spec_X(𝒜)` extends to a contravariant functor
269	`Spec_X : QcohAlg(X)^op ⥤ Over X`. The iter-173 file-skeleton encodes the
270	object-level functorial assignment as `X.QcohAlgebra → Over X`; the
271	morphism-level action and full `Functor` packaging are iter-174+ work after
272	`QcohAlgebra` is unpacked to a category.
273	
274	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_functorial` (Stacks 01LR
275	definition-relative-spec + lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec). -/
276	
277	/-- **The relative-spectrum functor (object level).**
278	
279	The object-level functorial action `𝒜 ↦ Over.mk (π_𝒜) : Over X`, packaging
280	the relative spectrum together with its structure morphism over `X`.
281	
282	iter-174+: the body is concrete via `Over.mk (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)`
283	but is left as `sorry` here because `RelativeSpec.structureMorphism` is itself
284	a typed `sorry`; once the structure morphism lands the body collapses to
285	`fun 𝒜 => Over.mk (structureMorphism 𝒜)`. The full categorical functor
286	`X.QcohAlgebra ⥤ Over X` (with `map` action induced by the universal property)
287	becomes expressible once `QcohAlgebra` carries its category structure. -/
288	noncomputable def functor (X : Scheme.{u}) :
289	    X.QcohAlgebra → Over X :=
290	  fun 𝒜 => Over.mk (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)
291	
292	end RelativeSpec
293	
294	end Scheme
295	
296	end AlgebraicGeometry

14	What is the right Mathlib-aligned structure shape for

21	      + qcoh predicate.
